Risk and compliance culture
As a financial institution, Axis Bank's capability-
building extends beyond sharpening performance and
productivity to fostering strong risk awareness, compliance
discipline, and ethical judgement. During the year, we
continued to invest in creating a more risk-conscious and
accountable organisation.
The Bank requires all employees up to the level of the
President to complete mandatory annual compliance
modules, covering areas such as KYC, Anti-Money
Laundering (AML), Information Security, Data Protection,
Prevention of Sexual Harassment (POSH), Business
Continuity Plan (BCP), and the Code of Conduct. These
certifications help reinforce regulatory discipline and
consistent behavioural standards across the organisation.
To strengthen the leadership mindset around risk, the
Bank delivered a targeted intervention for all Senior Vice
Presidents II (SVPIIs) and Executive Vice Presidents (EVPs),
using real-life decision-making scenarios. This was aimed
at building ethical clarity, accountability and leadership
responsibility in risk management.
We further enhanced the capacity of our assurance
functions through targeted training initiatives for Risk
and Compliance professionals. The academies of Risk
and Compliance provided self-directed, multi-tiered
learning opportunities to equip professionals with vital
functional expertise, while RBI-mandated certification
pathways deepened the technical proficiency. The focused
development of the Central Audit and Risk Officers
(CARO) cohort through blended learning modalities further
reinforced governance and assurance frameworks.
Integrated Annual Report 2025-26
F. O R E M P L O Y E E S S 5

Strengthening internal talent pool and leadership depth
Alongside external hiring and structured learning, we continued to deepen internal talent pools and create stronger
pathways for leadership progression through the following programs.
All Stars
Fiscal 2026 marked the introduction of All Stars, an
industry first talent identification and development
program for frontline sales personnel. The program is
designed to identify high-potential employees, build their
capabilities through intensive bootcamps and prepare them
for roles with expanded portfolios.
In its inaugural year, the program saw ~12,000 applicants,
of whom 465 were trained in customer management,
relationship building, portfolio management, digital and AI
skills, grooming, communication and professional presence.
Frontline employees to be trained in the next
fiscal through All Stars program
Astros
During the year, the Astros program continued to serve
as a structured acceleration pathway for mid-level leaders
identified through assessment. Participants undergo
specialised development through interactions with senior
leaders, workshops, projects, and peer networking,
and are fast-tracked for promotion with industry-
competitive compensation.
Fiscal 2026 saw the selection of the program s third
cohort, with 70 selects currently undergoing a curated
development journey. Participants in this program also
engage in career conversations with leaders and HR to
support long-term growth. ~200 Astros are now part of
the Enterprise AI Group s Evangelist program, in which
departmental representatives will build AI capabilities,
learn to identify use cases and work with the central
team to customise solutions. The program commences in
April 2026.
Mentorship
Our mentorship program serves as a structured and trusted
platform for leadership development across the Group.
The program includes ManCom members, Subsidiary CEOs
and several Group Heads as mentors, with select SVP I+
employees participating as mentees.
The platform is designed as a safe space where leaders
can discuss their aspirations, goals and challenges while
benefitting from the experience and guidance of senior
leaders. Each mentee develops four meaningful individual
connections during the year, with the structure and
cadence being shaped by the mentor-mentee relationship.
Mentees interacted with 73 mentors through the
Mentorship program in fiscal 2026

Embedding performance, fairness and a values-led culture
The Bank s performance philosophy is built on
transparency, objectivity, fairness and accountability.
Employees are assessed through clearly defined Key Result
Areas (KRAs), achievement targets, and developmental
feedback. Moderation committees chaired by senior
leaders play an important role in this assessment, ensuring
consistent, bias-free evaluations. Learning support is also
provided to managers to make performance conversations
and feedback more constructive and growth-oriented.
To ensure market competitiveness and pay fairness, the
Bank collaborates with Aon Consulting to benchmark
compensation, reward high performance through variable
pay and reinforce the principle of equal pay for equal work.
CEO compensation
The remuneration of the Bank s Managing Director and
Chief Executive Officer is in accordance with the directives
issued by the RBI on November 4, 2019. Performance is
evaluated using a Balanced Scorecard that covers Financial,
Internal Process, Compliance, People, Execution, and
Subsidiary metrics. Variable compensation includes a
performance-linked bonus and stock-linked incentives,
structured in line with regulatory requirements and
deferred payout provisions. A specific portion of the bonus
is deferred over three periods, while stock-linked incentives
vest over 48 months. All variable pay components are
subject to malus and clawback clauses in accordance with
the Bank s policies.
Values-led culture
Following the Values Refresh, the focus during the
year shifted to making values more visible in leadership
behaviour and team environments. This was enabled
through 54 Culture Councils, led by Department Heads,
which used survey insights to identify cultural gaps and
drive structured action plans.
Reimagining careers: Skills, mobility & growth
At Axis Bank, internal careers are a distinctive
organisational advantage - enabling us to build leaders who
understand our business, culture, and customers deeply.
We believe that leadership is cultivated by navigating
career opportunities and taking bold bets.
To enable this, we launched our Internal Mobility
proposition - thrive, designed to empower employees to
take charge of their careers, develop diverse skills and
think beyond traditional growth paths.
Our approach is anchored in an internal-first philosophy,
prioritising homegrown talent over external hiring, backed by
robust policies, aligned recruiter and business mindsets, and
an evolved operating model that rewards career progression
through year-round promotions and salary corrections.
Axis Bank is the first Indian Bank to introduce mid-year
promotions, reinforcing this commitment. This initiative
was launched with senior leadership sponsorship and active
engagement with business managers to drive behavioural
change across levels. In parallel, we are working to increase
employee participation on our platform, reaching the last
mile while navigating legacy processes and systems.
As we continue to expand, skills are emerging as the
foundational context layer powering this transformation.
The skills framework sits at the core of our HR ecosystem,
increasingly shaping performance conversations,
promotion recommendations, and feedback processes with
greater objectivity and precision - marking the next phase
of our shift towards a truly skills-based organisation.
At the heart of this evolution is our fully in-house talent
marketplace platform, built with skills as the foundational
layer, where every employee has a distinct profile defined
by unique skill signatures that enable more personalised,
data-driven talent decisions.

6,046
Promotions*
Driving cross-functional movements
As part of our internal mobility proposition, we encourage
cross-functional movements, enabling employees to transition
into roles adjacent to their current skill sets. This approach
helps employees acquire new skills, enhances the Bank s skill
intensity, and serves as a retention lever for those seeking
career pivots. 35% of selections this year are cross-functional,
fostering versatility and skill diversity across the Bank.

Creating a Workplace Where People Belong
Fiscal 2026 marked five years since the Bank formally
articulated its Diversity, Equity and Inclusion (DE&I) charter,
embedding inclusion as an organisational capability. Since
then, we have progressed from defining the foundational
principles to scaling implementation, strengthening
governance, measuring outcomes and integrating inclusion
more deeply into leadership practices, employee experience
and customer systems.
This journey has been guided by a clear ambition - to
achieve 30% women representation by fiscal 2027.
During the year, the Bank advanced this milestone by a
year, reflecting sustained progress and the strength of its
ecosystem-led approach.
At Axis Bank, the institutional foundations of inclusion
were laid through frameworks such as the DE&I Policy,
Human Rights Policy and ComeAsYouAre Charter. These
set the standards for equitable treatment, respectful
conduct and inclusive participation. In parallel, inclusion
capability was embedded early through onboarding and
induction, helping build awareness from the beginning of
the employee journey.
Evolving the language and capability of inclusion
Over time, the Bank s DE&I journey has evolved in a deliberate sequence:
Pause for Bias Gold strengthened the manager's
capability in applying inclusion standards to
everyday people's decisions
Pause for Bias encouraged employees to pause and
reflect, creating awareness around unconscious bias
Gateway to Inclusion reinforced intentional
inclusive behaviours across teams and leadership
routines awareness around unconscious bias
Every day, Inclusion sets the expectation
that inclusion must be demonstrated daily
through leadership conduct, team norms and
routine decisions
The number of Axis Value
Realisers (AVRs) increased from
2,339 to 2,658, reinforcing values-
led behaviour through broader
outreach and recognition.

Governance, leadership, ownership and
community building
The Bank strengthened governance through a DE&I
Council, enabling greater alignment, accountability and
follow-through across groups. Employee Resource Groups
(ERGs) continued to play an important role in building
community and shared ownership.
The Women in Sales ERG focused on improving women s
participation and progression in frontline roles through
sponsorship, mentoring and barrier removal. Pride365,
the LGBTQIA+ ERG, expanded from 7 members to over
1,000 members, strengthening allyship, advocacy and
community support.
Inclusive products and services
The Bank s inclusion journey also continued to shape
customer-facing propositions and product design.

f LGBTQIA+ inclusion: Since its launch in September
2021, the charter has enabled inclusive policies (dress
code, partner benefits, leave) and customer access
(joint accounts, nominees, 'Mx' title). In fiscal 2026,
digital enablement extended this further, allowing all
life partners, including queer partners, to access credit
cards online.

f Women s Banking Program: The program offers women
tailored financial solutions, lending support, travel
privileges, curated benefits linked to life-stage and
lifestyle needs, and improved access to credit. Together,
these initiatives reflect a broader belief that inclusion
must be experienced through access, recognition
and design.
External ecosystem engagement
Our DE&I agenda also extends beyond the organisation
through engagement with students, communities and
peer institutions.

f Students as future DE&I champions: The Bank s
DE&I curriculum on building and leading inclusive
organisations reached 700 students across 18
sessions nationwide.

f Axis Women in Motion: The program continues its
grassroots outreach across towns, hinterlands and
talukas to strengthen women s confidence, aspirations
and financial agency; over five years, the program
has reached 52,000 people through one-to-one and
virtual engagements.

f Axis VIBE (Varsity of Inclusive Business Enterprises)
Since 2022, this platform has reached 157 corporates
through events, seminars and formal engagements to
encourage inclusive business practices.
Research and thought leadership
The Bank continues to participate in broader conversations
on inclusion through research and thought leadership.

f Pink Capital: The Spectrum of Queer Money analysed
LGBTQIA+ participation across financial activities and
access barriers, emphasising inclusive documentation
and product design; the Bank serves over 7,000 gender-
diverse accounts who identified as transgender or
selected the gender neutral title 'Mx'

f The Missing Half: Women and India s Growth
Challenge', led by the Chief Economist, highlights
women s economic participation as a key growth
lever, drawing on global evidence and a survey of
~11,000 women across 42 cities to identify aspirations,
constraints, and system-level levers

to ensure employees feel heard, valued and supported.

Amber, the AI-driven conversation platform, engaged
more than 45,000 employees through over 87,000 chats,
now extending from Day 3 onboarding onward. The year
also saw the launch of Values Voices Survey, aligned to
the Bank s five core value and a refreshed ISAT Survey for
senior roles, using AI to derive deeper insights.

for redressal.

Caring for our people
During fiscal 2026, the Bank continued to invest in creating
an environment where employees feel supported and
empowered to thrive. The efforts included strengthening
access to preventive healthcare, improving financial
protection and expanding well-being support across
physical, emotional and family needs.
Physical well-being and health support
Axis Bank organised several health camps across India in
fiscal 2026, investing more than ₹3 crores in preventive
healthcare and proactive health management, and reaching
over 4,000 employees.
The year also saw enhancements to Group Medical
Coverage, with limits increased for maternity care, cataract
treatment, and ambulance services to reduce out-of-pocket
expenditure and strengthen the employee safety net.
A series of well-being webinars and health sessions
was conducted during the year to promote healthier
habits and greater awareness among employees. These
sessions covered a range of themes, including yoga, office
ergonomics, heart health & cholesterol, eye care, nutrition
and gut health.
Mental wellness ecosystem
The Bank s mental wellness ecosystem is designed to
ensure accessibility, confidentiality and a non-judgemental
experience, enabling employees and their families to
seek support with confidence. Experienced professionals
deliver care and are responsive to both personal and work-
related challenges.
In fiscal 2026, key elements of the mental wellness
ecosystem included the following:

f Queer-affirmative and inclusive care for LGBTQIA+
employees and their families, as well as support
for women, persons with disabilities and diverse
family structures

f Multiple therapeutic approaches, including dance
movement therapy and psychodrama, alongside
conventional counselling

f Group sessions on stress, burnout, grief and
emotional regulation

f Family support services covering relationships,
parenting, trauma and life transitions

Recognising contribution
Recognition remains an important lever for reinforcing
our organisational culture, for celebrating excellence and
strengthening a sense of belonging.
The Champions Awards, the Bank s highest recognition
platform, honoured employees across the Axis Group
who exemplified the organisation s core values. From over
15,000 stories, 117 winners were celebrated by senior
leaders at a gala event, with Mr Swaminathan Janakiraman,
RBI Deputy Governor, as the Chief Guest.
The Bank also continued to run quarterly recognition
platforms, such as Anchors, which recognised 3,590
employees for demonstrating ownership mantras, including
Own the Customer, Own the Brand, Act with Speed, and
Zero Pendency.
Through the OGS program, the Bank celebrated 5,913
employees who completed long-service milestones.
Leadership also honoured 105 employees who completed
25, 30 and 35-year milestones.
Additional recognition platforms included:

f Ahead of the Curve, through which we recognised
384 managers for promoting diversity, inclusion and
team empowerment

f Retiring with Pride, which honoured 32 retiring
employees for their contributions

f Squad Keepers, launched in fiscal 2026, which
recognised 12,364 managers who maintained zero
attrition in frontline teams every quarter, with 570
managers sustaining zero attrition through the
entire year

Employer of Choice, By Design
During the year, several recognitions confirmed the Bank s standing as an Employer of Choice.
Among the honours were the following:
Enabling a better employee experience through technology
Technology continues to enhance the employee experience
by making IT simpler, more responsive, and productive.
During the year, the HR Technology team made this
possible by ensuring stronger self-service, better process
controls, analytics and smarter decision support.
A major milestone was the launch of Adi, the Bank s GenAI-
powered HR virtual assistant. Designed to deliver accurate,
first-time-right responses to HR queries, Adi has ingested
more than 1,400 FAQs and 52 policies, significantly
expanding the depth and quality of self-service support.
There was a significant increase in the number of HR
queries handled by Adi during the year, establishing IT as
a primary HR self-service channel.
The Bank also strengthened process controls by automating
critical hiring checks such as PAN deduplication and credit
score verification. The One Axis App continued to provide
a unified self-service experience across the Bank and its
subsidiaries, enabling employees to access attendance,
leave management, travel and query resolution through
a single platform.
In parallel, there was sufficient progress in developing
an Employee Data Lake to centralise lifecycle data and
strengthen analytics across organisation design, headcount,
attrition, productivity, and KRA alignment. We also invested
in an AI-driven Talent Marketplace to support skill-based
internal mobility. Such initiatives are helping simplify
processes, improving decision-making and strengthening
the digital foundations for scale.

The Bank s people agenda is closely linked to its broader

integrity, ethics and ESG principles into HR practices, we
are building a workforce of responsible bankers who uphold
high standards of conduct, support inclusive growth,
and contribute to outcomes that are resilient, trusted
and enduring.
During the year, equity, inclusion and responsible leadership
became more deeply embedded across the people agenda.
We believe lasting value is created when performance is
grounded in trust, capability, fairness and integrity, and
our approach to people continues to reflect this focus with
clarity and discipline.

As India s growth deepens through domestic manufacturing, accelerating urbanisation,
digital payments, developing AI ecosystem, increasing rural development, GCC
expansion and rising aspirational consumption, Axis Bank is distinctly well-positioned to
enable this transformation by scaling premium and affluent banking, deepening Bharat
and MSME engagement, and driving digital banking through AI/tech-led efficiencies
across the franchise.
Sustainable wealth creation
for shareholders
As the global economy navigates increasing
geopolitical tensions, emerging financial
vulnerabilities, and a more fragmented and
risk-averse policy landscape, uncertainty
has become an inherent characteristic of
the international system. India, however,
approaches this phase from a position of
relative strength, supported by resilient
domestic demand, credible macroeconomic
buffers, a high degree of strategic autonomy,
and deeper integration into global markets
through recent trade partnerships.

This momentum is further reinforced by the Micro, Small, and Medium Enterprises (MSME) sector, long considered
the backbone of the Indian economy, which is gaining structural strength through formalisation, digital enablement,
and improved access to credit, thereby promoting inclusive and broad-based growth. Aligned with these shifts, Axis
Bank is well positioned to harness India s growth trajectory by combining its expansive physical franchise with best-
in-class digital and AI driven capabilities, delivering long term value for investors while contributing to broad based
economic development.

Shareholder wealth creation
The Bank has delivered strong, risk-adjusted returns, outperforming market indices over the near-and medium-term -
underscoring the effectiveness of its strategy focused on sustainable growth, disciplined capital allocation and prudent
risk management. Shareholder returns are further supported by a balanced capital allocation that rewards investors while
preserving financial flexibility for future growth. The Bank s Dividend Declaration Policy targets a minimum payout of 25%
of the equity share s face value from the net profit earned during the year, reflecting its commitment to providing stable
returns to shareholders while retaining sufficient capital to strengthen the balance sheet, invest in growth, and enhance
long-term intrinsic value.

Digital & innovation
Advances in AI and technology-driven disruption are
transforming finance by boosting productivity, refining
risk management, and enabling personalised, scalable
customer engagement.
At Axis Bank, we continue to develop and enhance our in-
house AI capabilities to empower employees via our super
app, 'Siddhi'. Our ongoing investments in technology, data
analytics, and digital infrastructure continue to strengthen
our competitive position. The Bank is also among the
first private banks certified for the Artificial Intelligence
Management System (AIMS), ensuring our commitment
to secure, ethical and accountable AI deployment across
Our digital-first approach is evident in strong customer
adoption and high ratings for our mobile app. The 'Sparsh'
initiative promotes a consistent service culture with
leadership and frontline involvement nationwide. While
leveraging traditional strengths, the Bank is leading India's
digital banking evolution, maintaining top position as a UPI
payer PSP, with lowest decline rates and strong market
share across merchant acquisition, credit card, NEFT and
IMPS transaction volumes, demonstrating the Bank s
execution capabilities in a digital-first economy.

Value protection
Capital allocation & risk
Axis Bank s approach to long term shareholder value
creation is anchored in disciplined stewardship, with a clear
focus on building a resilient and high quality franchise
across economic cycles. The Bank prioritises profitable
growth underpinned by prudent risk management, strong
capital and liquidity buffers, and a diversified earnings mix -
ensuring sustainability of returns rather than short term
optimisation. This discipline was evident in fiscal 2026,
when the Bank continued to gain market share in advances
despite a cautious industry wide slowdown in retail lending,
by selectively deepening its presence in corporate and
MSME segments while preserving the overall risk adjusted
returns (RAROC).
Central to this strategy is a sustained focus on operational
efficiency and productivity, driven by continued
investments in digital platforms, data led decision making,
and the responsible deployment of advanced analytics
and AI across processes. These initiatives enhance
scalability, strengthen customer engagement, and
improve cost efficiency, while reinforcing governance
and control frameworks. Despite opening 400 branches
in fiscal 2026, the Bank s total headcount declined by
3% and cost to assets improved by 18 bps y-o-y during
this fiscal, reflecting tangible gains in employee and
branch productivity.
With a strong capital position, including a CET 1 ratio of
14.38% which is ~638 bps (including capital conservation
buffer) above regulatory minimum, Axis Bank combines
balance sheet strength, execution discipline, and
technology enabled efficiency to remain firmly committed
to compounding shareholder value through consistent,
risk adjusted returns over the long-term.

F. O R I N V E S T O R S S 5

Governance and transparency
The Bank s Fair Disclosure Code provides a clear framework
to ensure timely, transparent, and equitable disclosure of
any Unpublished Price Sensitive Information (UPSI) that
could impact the market value of the Bank s securities.
In accordance with regulatory requirements, the Bank
ensures that such information is first disseminated through
stock exchanges, followed by other public platforms, to
ensure universal, non-selective access.
The Bank consistently shares key updates, including
financial results, corporate announcements, and material
developments, in accordance with the regulatory guidelines
set out in the Fair Disclosure Code. These disclosures are
designed to enable investors and market participants to
make informed decisions. Some of the disclosures available
on the Bank s website include:

f Quarterly earnings investor presentation, earnings press
release and financial results

f Earnings call transcripts and audio/video recordings

f Investor presentations for road shows, conferences and
specific events

f Investor presentations on specific businesses or
subsidiaries of the Bank
Stakeholder engagement
Shareholders form a core pillar of the Bank s growth
ecosystem, supporting both capital formation and long-
term strategic execution.
Likewise, Bondholders are a critical pillar of the Bank s
diversified capital structure, serving as long term partners
in funding growth and balance sheet resilience. The debt
capital market provides the Bank with flexible access to
non equity, non deposit funding, enabling the issuance
of tenor specific instruments and subordinated debt that
strengthens regulatory capital through Tier II qualification.
During fiscal 2026, the Bank successfully raised ₹5,000
crores through the issuance of long term Infrastructure
Bonds in India, aligned with funding requirements for
infrastructure and affordable housing. In the same
year, subordinated debt aggregating ₹1,500 crores and
Infrastructure Bonds amounting to ₹3,000 crores matured,
reflecting the Bank s disciplined liability management and
proactive refinancing strategy.
We engage proactively with our shareholders and
bondholders through disciplined, transparent, and timely
communication, fostering long term partnerships built on
trust and clarity.
The Bank conducts regular interactions through a
structured mix of in-person and virtual meetings, including:

f Post-quarterly earnings calls, the Annual General
Meeting and analysts meetings

f Domestic and international conferences and non-
deal roadshows

f One-on-one and group meetings
Consistent and meaningful engagement enhances market
understanding of the Bank s strategy and performance,
strengthens price discovery and underpins fair valuation -
contributing to sustained investor confidence over the
long term.

As one of India s leading financial institutions, our purpose extends well beyond
banking. We believe meaningful progress is achieved only when communities flourish
- when opportunity, dignity and well-being are not distant ideals, but everyday
realities for every Indian. Anchored in the Anchored in our ethos of 'dil se open',
we approach community development with empathy, openness and an enduring
commitment to inclusion.
Enabling inclusive,
lasting progress
At the heart of our CSR strategy lies a deep
focus on unlocking human potential and
building long-term resilience, especially for
underserved and marginalised communities.
From strengthening rural livelihoods and
expanding access to quality education in
difficult-to-reach regions, to empowering
women through financial literacy and
economic participation, each initiative is
a deliberate step towards a more just and
equitable India.

Our CSR programs are supported by a robust institutional
governance framework. They are guided by the CSR
Committee of the Board and fully aligned with Section
135 of the Companies Act, 2013. We remain committed
to global best practices, drawing inspiration from the
United Nations Sustainable Development Goals (UN
SDGs) to ensure our initiatives create lasting social and
environmental impact.
In fiscal 2026, our work spanned diverse geographies and
thematic areas - from border villages to urban informal
settlements; from natural resource management and
climate resilience to cancer care, grassroots sports, and
academic research. Each milestone we achieved was
underpinned by a shared purpose and the collective effort
of communities, partners and institutions committed to
driving meaningful change.

Lives & livelihoods
In rural India, people's livelihoods are deeply connected to
local economies, community ties, natural resources, and
traditions. While rural communities contribute significantly
to agriculture, livestock, dairy, textiles, artisanal production
and essential public services-the benefits of economic
progress remain uneven across households. Shrinking
landholdings, climate change, water shortage, limited
market access and skill gaps restrict opportunities. Coupled
with changing aspirations and economic pressures, these
challenges are leading to migration and underemployment,
particularly among the youth.
Established in 2011 by Axis Bank Foundation, the
Sustainable Livelihood Program (SLP) is structured to
empower rural households by enhancing their capacity,
improving access, and fostering sustained engagement
towards the betterment of their lives and livelihoods. The
program operates across diverse geographies nationwide
with a focus on aspirational districts, rainfed regions and
other underserved areas where poverty is more prevalent.
IT works with small and marginal farmers, landless, tribal
regions, women, youth, artisans and disadvantaged groups
through context-specific interventions.
SLP combines traditional knowledge with modern practices
to create sustainable livelihood opportunities. IT promotes
capacity-building through training, exposure visits, peer-to-
peer learning, and community institutions. To complement
capacity building, IT aims at improving access to technology,
markets, finance, government welfare schemes and
advanced agricultural techniques. By strengthening
linkages with local institutions such as Panchayati Raj
Institutions (PRIs), three-tier institutions promoted by the
National Rural Livelihood Mission, with market actors and
other stakeholders, the program helps communities build
resilience, enhance incomes and achieve long-term socio-
economic progress.
SLP s integrated model
SLP applies a three-pronged approach of Capacity, Access, and Engagement across various key livelihood areas including
natural resource management, farm productivity, livestock management, enterprise development and health.
45,000+ hectares of land
Brought under watershed management
1.2 million
Trees were planted through afforestation and
horticulture
3,500+
Major and minor lift irrigation systems were made
functional
2,500 hectares
Of pastureland were restored
Natural resource management
SLP strengthens community-led management through
village-level or watershed-level planning while improving
access through integration with Panchayati Raj institutions
and promoting collective action for effective resource
stewardship. Demonstrated outcomes include improved
water accessibility, enhanced soil moisture conservation,
afforestation, development of pasture lands, increased
availability of fodder and water for livestock, formation
of collaborative user groups, fair access for all users, and
consistent maintenance over time.
Fiscal 2026 highlights
Integrated Annual Report 2025-26
F. O R C O M M U N I T I E S S 5

Farm productivity
Improved access to water enables higher cropping
intensity, supporting multiple harvest cycles and resulting
in increased farm productivity. This is further reinforced
through crop diversification, more reliable water availability,
the introduction of horticulture and floriculture, improved
seed quality, and weather-appropriate crop selection.
Capacity building is complemented by access to locally
produced organic manure and seeds, innovative cultivation
techniques, adoption of small tools and micro-irrigation
systems, and a shift to renewable energy. Farmer
engagement is strengthened through knowledge-sharing
platforms, collective procurement of inputs, aggregation of
produce for sale, and value addition activities that improve
market access and price realisation.
Fiscal 2026 highlights
3.68 lakhs+
Households supported with livelihood planning
3.45 lakhs+
Households supported for Kharif (monsoon farming)
4.19 lakhs+
Households supported for Rabi (winter farming)
Livestock development
Livestock is integral to rural lives and in some regions
households have traditionally reared livestock. SLP builds
community capacity in breed improvement, animal health,
feeding practices and livestock management. Access is
strengthened through linkages to the public veterinary care
system, quality feed suppliers and higher-value markets.
The program also promotes collective action to purchase
feed, manage pasture lands for better water and fodder
access, and for aggregated sales.
Fiscal 2026 highlights
2 lakhs+ households
Supported to improve livelihoods through livestock

Artisanal livelihoods
In rural areas, artisans represent the second-largest
workforce after farmers. While traditional crafts have lost
some of their functional relevance, growing appreciation
for handcrafted products has created new market
opportunities for artisans.
SLP supports artisans through product innovation, design
development, trend integration through incorporation of
seasonal colour palettes, enhancement of quality standards,
and improvement of production capacity. IT helps artisans
develop competencies in product design, market analysis,
customer engagement, and digital tool utilisation. These
measures are progressively strengthening or revitalising
income prospects and encouraging younger generations to
consider traditional craft as a sustainable career path.
Fiscal 2026 highlights
5,000+
Artisans supported
Rural entrepreneurship
Interest in self-employment is rapidly growing among
rural youth. Anticipating increased demand for non-
traditional goods and services in rural areas, SLP has
been supporting aspiring entrepreneurs to set up small
businesses, access various government schemes for
subsidies, conduct market research, product and service
testing, and develop business plans. IT has also been
equipping them with financial literacy, competition analysis,
unit economics, and fundamental management skills
necessary to start or operate profitable enterprises while
enabling them to engage with other entrepreneurs through
supportive networks.
Fiscal 2026 highlights
21,000+
Entrepreneurs supported
Integrated Annual Report 2025-26
F. O R C O M M U N I T I E S S 5

Health and well-being
Good health is fundamental to overall well-being and
resilient livelihoods, particularly in rural India, where
poor health outcomes remain closely linked to persistent
intergenerational poverty, especially among women. SLP
promotes strengthened health-seeking behaviour through
awareness generation, better access to public health
systems, and deeper engagement with frontline providers
across various life stages and groups, including infants,
pregnant women, young mothers, adolescents, and youth.
The program is enabling this shift and facilitating intentional
change toward better health consciousness.

Sanitation and Hygiene (WASH) initiatives
~5,000
Health camps organised
~22,000
Households linked with various health schemes
Mission 4 Million
Rural challenges are dynamic and interconnected, requiring
responses that go beyond short-term support. Building on
two completed phases, SLP has now entered its Mission
4 Million phase, with a sharper focus on scale and depth.
The program seeks to strengthen local capacities, deepen
institutional linkages, and enable meaningful community
participation to build resilient, adaptive rural systems that
can sustain long-term improvements in incomes and quality
of life.

From unemployment to entrepreneurship
At 29, Manoj from Uttar Pradesh faced multiple challenges. Living with a locomotor disability and caring for
his mother, who has paralysis, he struggled to find a sustainable source of income despite completing Class XII.
Limited awareness of suitable livelihood opportunities kept him unemployed for a prolonged period, placing
significant strain on the household.
His journey towards self-employment began when he was introduced to a project under the Sustainable
Livelihood Program. Through a 10-day Entrepreneurship Development Program, Manoj gained foundational
business skills and exposure to viable livelihood options. Post training, he received support to start an ironing
service in his village and connected with a local entrepreneur for mentorship and handholding.
As his enterprise gained community acceptance, he expanded by opening a small cosmetics shop alongside his
ironing service. This diversification has strengthened his income, enabled better medical care for his mother,
and improved his confidence. Today, Manoj stands as a self-reliant individual and a role model for others in
his community.
Education: Learning with heart, learning for life
Education remains one of the most powerful catalysts for unlocking human potential and driving long-term community
transformation. At Axis Bank, we recognise education not merely as a fundamental right, but as a pathway to dignity,
opportunity, and self-reliance - particularly for children and youth across 20+ states, including some of India s most remote
and underserved regions.

F. O R C O M M U N I T I E S S 5

Our education portfolio follows a three-pillar approach that addresses key barriers across the
learning lifecycle while strengthening access, quality, and outcomes:
Three pillars of Education
Axis DilSe - Access and enabling infrastructure
Launched in 2017, Axis DilSe is our flagship program,
committed to transforming education in India s most
remote and underserved regions. What began with
early investments in difficult geographies has evolved
into a multi-state initiative across the Northeast and
other aspirational regions.
Rooted in our dil se open philosophy, the program
works closely with grassroots organisations and
institutions to strengthen access, improve school
ecosystems, and enable pathways for students to
realise their aspirations. Through residential learning,
school transformation, and community engagement,
Axis DilSe is ensuring that children in geographically
isolated regions are not left behind in India s
growth story.
Through Axis DilSe , the Bank supports Centres of
Excellence across the Northeast, providing structured,
residential coaching for competitive examinations to
underserved youth, with centres supporting 120-280
students over multi-year cycles.
This is complemented by holistic schooling models
such as the Hummingbird School in Majuli, which
provides inclusive residential education to 300+ tribal
students annually, including hostel support.
School transformation and ecosystem strengthening
initiatives with partners such as Sunbird Trust and
Tata Steel Foundation have enabled infrastructure
upgrades, foundational learning interventions, and
community engagement, reaching over 80,000
students across 800+ schools and 950+ anganwadis
in remote geographies.

System strengthening
Our interventions focus on strengthening public
education systems at scale by improving learning
outcomes, teacher capacity, and classroom practices
through structured, long-term partnerships with state
governments and institutions.
Large-scale programs have driven
measurable impact:

f In Odisha, digital maths and science learning is
reaching 4.86 lakhs students across 6,400+ schools,
supported by 18,600+ teachers through curriculum-
aligned digital content

f In Madhya Pradesh, the CM Rise Teacher
Development Program is targeting ~3 lakhs
teachers across all 52 districts, with downstream
impact on ~90 lakhs children

f District-led initiatives such as Shikshagraha have
reached 3.76 lakhs students and 9,200+ educators,
demonstrating improvements in school readiness
and foundational learning

f The Rupantar program has impacted 26,000+
teachers and 3.5 lakhs students, enabling a shift
towards competency-based, experiential learning
At the community level, focused interventions such
as Yellow Rooms and Muheem Leader Fellowship in
UP with a collective reach of 3,500+ students, LIFI
in Madhya Pradesh and Haryana reaching ~ 1,000
students, and English Bridge Program in Chhattisgarh
with a reach of 12,000 students are strengthening
foundational literacy, inclusion, and retention across
high-need geographies.

Higher education, research and innovation
We support academic excellence through long-
term investments in higher education institutions,
research ecosystems, and scholarships, contributing to
innovation and future-ready talent for the country.
Key initiatives include:

f The Axis Bank Centre for Mathematics &
Computing at IISc, supporting 61 scholars and
engaging over 2,100 participants through advanced
research and academic programs

f The FutureTech Building at Plaksha University,
enabling cutting-edge work in AI, robotics, and
emerging technologies, alongside PhD and faculty-
led research

f Partnerships with Ashoka University, supporting
118 PhD scholars and 13 postdoctoral fellows,
and strengthening interdisciplinary science and
research capabilities

f Scholarships across Plaksha, Ashoka and ISDM,
enabling access to quality higher education and
leadership pathways
Together, these pillars enable a continuum of learning
- from strengthening foundational education in
remote and border villages to supporting higher
education, research, and innovation - thus helping
learners realise their full potential.
Our efforts in education are aimed at not only to help
children learn but also towards enabling communities
to aspire, participate, and lead. By investing across
access, systems, and excellence, Axis Bank is building
an education ecosystem where every learner,
regardless of geography or background, has the
opportunity to thrive.

Well being - A comprehensive approach to resilience
Beyond Sustainable Livelihoods and Education, our CSR efforts are designed to address
interconnected social and environmental challenges that influence the overall well-being
and resilience of communities.
Financial inclusion & literacy

f Financial literacy is a critical pillar of our CSR
strategy, with a strong emphasis on enhancing
women s economic participation and financial
capability to enable greater agency, self-reliance,
and long-term financial security. Our initiatives
reached ~2.6 lakhs people across India, including
the empowerment of nearly 1.6 lakhs women
through our partnership with Kalanjiam Foundation

f In addition, our teams conducted ~2,200 financial
literacy and awareness programs and launched
the FI Model Village initiative across 80 villages,
while digitising ~1,000 SHGs to promote cashless
transactions and deepen financial inclusion
Health

f We adopt a systems-oriented, infrastructure-
led, and patient-centric approach, prioritising
cancer care, mental health, paediatric and
developmental healthcare, and specialised tertiary
services. Recognising that sustainable health
outcomes depend on strong institutions, trained
professionals, digital systems, and enabling
care environments our interventions in fiscal
2026 focused on long-term capacity building,
institutional strengthening, and creation of critical
healthcare infrastructure instead of short-term
service delivery

f Through our health initiatives, we reached over
~16,000 direct participants - including patients,
children in institutional care, caregivers, healthcare
professionals, and students - and indirectly
benefitted over ~1.2 crores individuals through
mass awareness initiatives and systemic, national-
level interventions

f The interventions spanned Maharashtra, Uttar
Pradesh, Bihar, Tamil Nadu, Karnataka, West
Bengal, and pan-India national programs
Environmental sustainability

f Under this theme, we focus on restoring,
conserving, and sustainably managing natural
ecosystems while strengthening community
resilience to climate variability and resource
stress through a landscape based, community led,
and science backed approach. In fiscal 2026,
the portfolio expanded to include rural water
security and watershed based interventions,
recognising water as a critical ecological and
livelihood resource

f During the year, we planted approximately 15 lakhs
saplings and restoring nearly 3,000 hectares of
land, creating dense, community managed urban
green patches and promoting climate resilient,
native species in rural landscapes to improve soil
health, water retention, and ecosystem resilience.
Interventions also supported habitat restoration,
agroforestry, soil and water conservation, and
biodiversity protection - particularly in buffer
zones of national parks and wildlife sanctuaries -
to strengthen ecosystems, support livelihoods and
reduce human-wildlife conflict

f Climate resilience efforts included solar-powered
cold chain infrastructure to reduce post-harvest
losses and flood resilience measures in riverine
regions. Anchored around ecosystem restoration,
biodiversity conservation, water security, climate
resilience, community stewardship, and science-led
planning, the theme program reached over
2 lakh direct participants across 13 states

Sports

f Continuing its focus on sports development, Axis
Bank supported athletes across high-performance

f The Axis Bank Judo Development Program, in
collaboration with the Inspire Institute of Sport
(IIS), provided foundational training to 560 judokas
in Manipur and advanced, fully-funded residential
training to 47 female judokas at the IIS campus in
Karnataka, targeting LA 2028 podium outcomes.
Additionally, Axis Bank partnered with Lakshya
Shooting Club to establish a state-of-the-art High
Performance Centre in Navi Mumbai to develop an
emerging and elite shooting talent
Disaster response and community resilience

f Under the Humanitarian & Relief theme,
we provide timely, need-based support to
communities affected by climate-induced disasters,
particularly floods and cloudbursts, with a focus
on reducing immediate vulnerability and enabling
early rehabilitation

f In fiscal 2026, interventions were concentrated
on flood-affected hill and plain regions, addressing
urgent needs such as food security, temporary
shelter, winter protection, sanitation, and essential
household support. Dry ration kits were provided
together with warm clothing, insulated temporary
shelters, sanitation facilities and school supplies,
with priority given to the most vulnerable groups

f Overall, the interventions reached ~6,000+ directly
affected people across Uttarakhand, Himachal
Pradesh and Punjab
Corporate citizenship initiatives
Axis Cares
Axis Cares is the Bank s flagship employee
engagement platform that fosters a culture of
empathy and encourages Axisians to contribute
meaningfully to society. Rooted in the values of
giving back and personal social responsibility,
IT provides employees with avenues to support
causes they care about through both financial
contributions and volunteering.
Over the years, Axisians have extended their
support across a wide range of social and
environmental causes, including education for
children, environmental and wildlife conservation,
care for senior citizens, preservation of cultural
heritage, promotion of sports and disaster relief
efforts. Employees have also actively contributed
their time by mentoring and coaching young
aspirants preparing to enter the workforce.
Axis Cares reflects a shared commitment among
Axisians to make a positive difference, reinforcing
the Bank s belief in collective responsibility and
purposeful engagement beyond the workplace.

National development requires institutions that are reliable and responsive. Axis
Bank collaborates with central and state governments to strengthen public finance,
service delivery and digital infrastructure, thereby improving outcomes for citizens.

A trustworthy, reliable partner
The Bank s Government Business promotes inclusive
growth, transparency, and digital transformation.
Collaborating with state and central governments, IT
facilitates sustainable public finance aligned with initiatives
such as Digital India and national priorities such as financial
inclusion and ESG. The Bank continues to expand its
delivery capabilities and broaden its national footprint to
meet growing banking needs, while investing in technology
to automate and digitise processes, improving efficiency.
The Bank is committed to advancing the government
banking sector and reimagining customer experiences
through a range of tailored products and services.
Currently, the Bank has the mandate to collect direct taxes, state taxes, GST, customs duty, railway
e-freight, civil, defence and railway pensions, Public Provident Fund (PPF), Sukanya Samriddhi Scheme,
and Kisan Vikas Patra. The Bank is among the top performers in the Public Financial Management
System (PFMS) and is an active participant in the Government E-Marketplace (GeM).

Driving Government Business through distinctive beyond banking proposition

Transforming agriculture through Digi-culture
Enabling smarter urban transformation
As a strategic partner to India s ambitious Smart City
Mission, the Bank collaborates closely with municipal
bodies, city administrations, and state governments to
deliver tailor-made banking solutions that meet local needs.
We maintain strategic relationships with over half of India's
Urban Local Bodies (ULBs) to support urban governance.
Our solutions that digitise and streamline municipal
operations ensure seamless payment and collection
processes, thereby enhancing transparency and civic
engagement. By integrating digital technology into public
service delivery, we help cities become more responsive,
sustainable and inclusive.
Among the solutions are:

f Integrated digital services platform enabling efficient
citizen services across ULBs

f QR code-based doorstep property tax solution

f App-based platform that activates digital certifications,
handles grievances, manages revenue and provides
social security services

f Drone-based aerial solutions to strengthen disaster
response and urban planning

f Digital door-numbering solution, enhancing service
delivery, responsiveness and community inclusion
We empower this vital sector through reliable, transparent,
and technologically advanced solutions that create ease
and convenience for farmers and stakeholders within
the agricultural value chain. Our premier digital platform
for electronic procurement facilitates secure, prompt
payments to farmers in accordance with government
procurement regulations. Engineered to manage large-scale
disbursements accurately, the platform provides real-time
reconciliation, bulk transfer functionalities, and a fully
automated interface that minimises delay, mitigates fraud
and fosters trust.
Implementing Direct Benefit Transfers
(DBT) ensures that every rupee
reaches the intended recipients
on time.
Our agricultural engagement spans key states, where we
collaborate with procurement agencies and cooperative
organisations to deploy scalable and effective solutions.
Key engagements in the agricultural sector

f Uttar Pradesh Upbhokta Sahakari Sangh

f Food & Civil Supplies Corporation - in West Bengal,
Madhya Pradesh, and Tamil Nadu

f Bihar State Vegetable Processing & Marketing
Cooperative Federation

f Andhra Pradesh State Farmers eVikraya Corporation

F. O R P A R T N E R S - G O V E R N M E N T S 5

Health & education
We support government institutions with banking solutions
and technology-enabled platforms that enhance efficiency
and citizen access. In healthcare, we introduced AI-based
facial recognition and HRMS software for Bihar's Health
Department to address issues such as absenteeism. This
initiative was replicated in Puducherry and Dadra &
Nagar Haveli under the National Health Mission. We also
collaborate on health projects, such as Gujarat's Ayushman
Bharat Health Infrastructure Mission, contributing to the
development of inclusive health systems.
In the education sector, we enable secure and seamless
transactions through digitised fee-collection platforms for
various state boards and institutions. We also provide fee
automation, reporting dashboards, and customised banking
products to schools, colleges, and universities, including the
Directorate of Education, Goa.
Driving digital mobility infrastructure
We partner with municipal authorities to facilitate efficient
and future-oriented vehicular movement. In Guwahati,
we inaugurated India s first AI and FASTag-enabled
smart parking system in partnership with the Guwahati
Metropolitan Development Authority, thereby eliminating
manual ticketing, enabling contactless entry and exit, and
establishing a new standard in urban mobility.
DBT initiatives
The Bank plays an essential role in enhancing India s
Direct Benefit Transfer (DBT) ecosystem by connecting
government welfare initiatives directly with their intended
beneficiaries, ensuring the timely, transparent delivery
of funds. Our DBT platforms support a diverse array
of state and centrally sponsored schemes, including
programs for women and child development, tribal welfare
and urban transformation missions. Through seamless
integration, real-time validation, and direct account
crediting, we prevent leakages and uphold the dignity of
social assistance.
DBT schemes handled

f Krantijyoti Savitribai Phule Bal Sangopan Yojana -
Women & Child Development Department, Maharashtra

f Chief Minister s Atmanirbhar Asom Abhijan - Assam

f Department of Adi Dravidar and Tribal Welfare -
Tamil Nadu

f AMRUT (Atal Mission for Rejuvenation and
Urban Transformation)

f Swachh Bharat Mission (SBM)
In Delhi, we became the exclusive
acquiring bank for RFID-based
FASTag fee collection at Inter-State
Bus Terminals (ISBTs), streamlining
fee collection, reducing congestion,
enhancing transparency, and
promoting cashless transportation.
The Bank aims to be a trusted partner in
nation-building by using technology, scale, and
governance to grow sustainable government
business. The focus remains on digitisation,
inclusion, and transparency to create long-term
stakeholder value.

Environmental stewardship is key to creating lasting value. IT guides us in our endeavour
to finance responsible growth, strengthen resilience and reduce our operational
footprint. Through disciplined capital allocation, climate-aware risk management and
resource-conscious operations, we support the transition to a low-carbon economy
while delivering sustainable value for our stakeholders.

future

India s development in line with the United

responsible capital, climate risk management,

and helped strengthen the enterprise risk

F. O R T H E E N V I R O N M E N T S 6
Integrated Annual Report 2025-26

Advancing low-carbon
operations
Energy and emissions
Aligned with India s climate goals and global
standards, we target a 3.5% reduction in per-
employee emissions by fiscal 2027, using fiscal
2023 as the baseline. Our decarbonisation
approach focuses on measurable action across
direct operations, purchased electricity, and
select indirect activities to enhance efficiency
and resilience.
We manage emissions across Scope 1, 2, and
3. Scope 1 emissions are tracked through
monthly monitoring of diesel expenditure and
refrigerant leaks, while Scope 2 emissions
are evaluated by tracking monthly electricity
usage and tariff data. Scope 3 impacts, such as
air travel and paper use, are addressed through
digital solutions.
Our key environmental impact arise from energy consumption at our branches, diesel generator
and paper usage, which we have significantly reduced through digitalisation, improved
operational efficiency and investments in green technologies.

F. O R T H E E N V I R O N M E N T S 6

Water conservation
We recognise water as an invaluable resource, essential for sustaining life and our daily operations. We actively implement
measures to conserve water through efficient management, recycling and responsible use practices. Through rainwater
harvesting and innovative recycling solutions, we seek to minimise our water footprint and create a positive impact on the
environment and the communities around us.
Key actions include

f Implementation of a closed-loop water system at the
Bengaluru Data Centre to eliminate water usage in
cooling operations

f Rainwater harvesting infrastructure at Axis
House, Mumbai

f Sewage treatment facilities that recycle water and
reduce freshwater demand

f Sensor-based washbasins, aerators and bio-blocks at
select offices to reduce wastage
Key achievements in fiscal 2026
~2.0 KL
Rainwater harvested annually
at Bengaluru Data Centre
~5.5 KL
Rainwater harvested annually
at Axis House Mumbai
1.
All emission calculations, including intensity assessments, energy
calculations with intensity considerations, water consumption, and
waste estimations, encompass solely Axis Bank s operations within
India. These calculations exclude any subsidiaries.
2. The reporting boundary for waste management is limited to 12
offices for Paper, 6 offices for Plastic, 4 offices for Metal, 1 office
for Battery waste, 12 offices for organic waste, 2 offices for C&D
Waste and E-waste reported for pan-India basis.
3. Scope 1 - Diesel-related emissions are calculated on estimation
basis and Scope 1 - AC - fugitive emissions are calculated on
estimation basis. Similarly, Scope 2 emissions are calculated basis
unit level consumption and expenditure.
Responsible waste management
We continue to strengthen responsible waste management practices through waste segregation, recycling initiatives, and
digital solutions across our operations. Our approach is designed to reduce environmental impact, divert waste away from
landfills and support a circular economy.
Key actions include

f Centralised systems at Axis House, Mumbai and select
major offices across India for segregation and recycling
of dry waste into usable stationery

f Collaboration with certified vendors for safe disposal of
electronic waste from branches and offices nationwide

f Digital banking initiatives, including digital account
opening, e-statements and e-welcome kits, to
significantly reduce paper consumption

Habitat restoration
As part of the Bank s ESG commitments, we have
set an ambitious target of planting 8 million trees
by 2030. As of March 2026, through our CSR
initiative, the Bank has planted approximately
4.16 million trees across 9 geographies in India,
including all its plantation programs. This reflects
our effort to create environmental value that
extends beyond our immediate operations and
contributes to a more resilient future.

Investing in a sustainable future
As a leading Indian financial institution, we are committed
to advancing the nation s development priorities
in alignment with the United Nations Sustainable
Development Goals (UN SDGs) and India s obligations
under the Paris Agreement. Our investments focus on
sectors that foster inclusive growth while promoting
environmental stewardship backed by enabling policies and
strong oversight in place.
Since 2021, the Board-level ESG Committee has provided
strategic oversight of climate-and broader ESG risks,
guiding the continued evolution of our sustainability
agenda. With strong Board direction, and led by the
Risk Management Department, we are strengthening
enterprise-wide climate risk management capabilities and
embedding climate considerations into key risk processes.
This proactive approach enhances operational resilience
and enables us to leverage opportunities arising from the
transition to a more sustainable economy.
Introduced in August 2021, our Sustainable Financing
Framework, aligned to the UN SDGs, serves as a
structured approach for ESG-linked issuances. IT was
the first such framework by an Indian bank to obtain a
Second Party Opinion. Recognising the environmental
and social implications of its operations, the Bank embeds
Environmental, Social and Governance (ESG) considerations
into its investment decision-making through its ESG Policy
for Lending.
Managing ESG and climate-related risks
Climate related risks are fast emerging as significant
financial risks with implications for businesses, households,
financial institutions and the wider economy. Driven by
both physical climate impacts and the urgency to transition
to a lower carbon economy, these risks, transmitted
through multiple channels, require an integrated risk
management approach as they can potentially affect
business profitability, household wealth and overall financial
stability and may also result in stranded assets.
For financial institutions, climate risks can manifest as
elevated credit, market, underwriting, operational and
liquidity risks, resulting in higher default rates, changes in
asset valuations, gaps in insurance coverage, operational
disruptions and increased pressure on liquidity.
Focus areas of actions undertaken under the Risk functions
Governance
Strengthening Board-level oversight on
climate and ESG risks
Climate and ESG risks are integrated into the Bank s risk
taxonomy and governed under the oversight of the Risk
Committee of the Board, supported by guidance and
oversight of the ESG Committee. The Bank continues to
enhance the Committee s governance and supervision
over these risks and embed them further within its Risk
Management Framework. Under the ESG Policy for
Lending framework, key proposals are also reviewed by
the Committee of Directors of the Board, within the Bank s
overall risk management strategy.
Policy and controls
Inclusion of ESG and climate-related risks
and opportunities in ICAAP
The Internal Capital Adequacy Assessment Process
evaluates ESG and climate-related risks as material risks,
covering both physical risk and transition risk.
Strengthening the ESG Policy for Lending
Since 2015, the Bank s ESG Policy for Lending incorporates
environmental and social risk assessment into its credit
appraisal process in alignment with the IFC Performance
standards and other pertinent global standards. The
implementation of the policy is overseen by the specialised
Environmental and Social Management Group within the
Credit function.
Integrated Annual Report 2025-26
F. O R T H E E N V I R O N M E N T S 6

Adoption of the Sustainable Financing
Framework
The Sustainable Financing Framework, launched in 2021 in
alignment with global standards such as the International
Capital Market Association (ICMA) Principles and the
Green Loan Principles, and with a Second Party Opinion
from Sustainalytics. The Bank has in place an ESG Working
Group that works under the ESG Committee s oversight
to ensure effective management of its sustainable
financing activities.
Risk toolkits
In-house ESG rating model to support credit
assessment
Piloted in fiscal 2023, the ESG rating model has been
adopted as part of the credit appraisal process for select
Wholesale Banking clients. Developed internally in close
alignment with the International Finance Corporation (IFC)
performance standards, IT covers 80+ parameters across
ESG pillars and reinforces the integration of E, S and G
considerations in the Bank s lending practices. During
the current fiscal, the model was further strengthened to
deepen borrower-level ESG assessment.
Building capabilities for stress testing
The Bank is developing stress testing and scenario
analysis capabilities in line with regulatory guidance and
supervisory expectations. External best practices are
also being studied to accelerate learning and strengthen
internal preparedness.
Development of climate risk dashboards
Since last fiscal, the Bank has been enhancing its climate
risk-related disclosures by expanding coverage of key
physical risks and transition risk metrics as well as lending
and financing activities aligned with its Sustainable
Financing Framework. As part of the dashboard, the Bank
actively tracks its transition risk exposures, highlighting
asset quality in identified sectors that IT deems to have
elevated transition risks such as coal and thermal power.
IT also assesses physical risks across retail, rural and SME
portfolios against four major natural hazards in India,
namely cyclones, droughts, heat waves and floods. The
climate risk dashboard is kept updated and presented to the
ESG Committee through the year.
Capacity building
Bank-wide ESG education and participation
During the year, the Bank partnered with Deutsche
Gesellschaft für Internationale Zusammenarbeit (GIZ)
GmbH, along with IPC GmbH and AuctusESG, to
strengthen its capacity in climate adaptation finance. The
capacity building program covered representatives from
the Bank s Wholesale Banking Coverage, Risk, Treasury,
Sustainability, and Credit teams who participated in a 2
day, intensified training workshop aimed at building an
understanding of climate adaptation finance to be able to
integrate its principles into the Bank s risk management
and lending practices. The program also facilitated cross-
functional collaboration, enhanced internal capabilities
to identify climate vulnerabilities, and approaches for
incorporating adaptation considerations into credit
appraisal and portfolio monitoring processes. This
collaboration also supports stronger institutional readiness
for climate-resilient lending and aligned to India s wider
global collaboration on sustainable development.
Engagement with peers and regulators
The Bank actively engages with industry peers and
regulators on matters relating to ESG and climate-related
risks, and sustainable finance. The Bank s senior leadership
participates in pertinent domestic ESG- and climate-
related committees and formal working groups, including
those under the RBI, Indian Banks Association, CII and
FICCI. During the current fiscal, the Bank participated in
an in-depth engagement with the regulator on physical
risk assessment data and methodology under the Reserve
Bank Climate Risk Information System. The Bank submitted
its inputs and responses to the regulator as part of
this engagement.

ESG Policy for Lending
Formulated in 2015, our ESG Policy for Lending is aligned to the IFC performance standards and other pertinent
international standards and benchmarks. IT is applied to various financial products, including project loans,
corporate loans, lines of credit, bridge loans, overseas funding, consortium/multiple banking, syndicated loans, and
refinance transactions, subject to internal criteria and threshold limits. The Policy also includes an Exclusion List
listing the themes and activities the Bank refrains from lending to.

The categorisation of cases reflects the status as of March 31, 2026. IT is to be noted that due to material changes in the proposal or project, the
categorisation of cases can also change over the duration of the loan.
In fiscal 2022, projects were categorised as A, B+, B, C, FI under our ESG Policy for Lending. We undertook a self-assessment of the policy under the IFC s ESMS
diagnostic tool, which benchmarks environmental and social management systems (such as our ESG Policy for Lending) against IFC s performance standards and
global best practices. The tool evaluates based on nine parameters, such as policy, due diligence, governance, capacity, and the control environment, categorising
them as State of the Art , Developed , and Emerging . Our ESG Policy for Lending scored State of the Art on four parameters and Developed on three.
The summary of project categories and due diligence under the policy are accessible:

F. O R T H E E N V I R O N M E N T S 6

Cases highlighting Environmental and Social Due Diligence (ESDD)
conducted under the ESG Policy for Lending
Environmental and social
risk assessment
in e-bus portfolio
Axis Bank has financed electric buses
(e-buses) and charging infrastructure to
support intra-city public transport across
multiple states. While aligned with low-
carbon mobility, the Bank conducted a
comprehensive Environmental and Social
(E&S) risk assessment to strengthen
portfolio risk management.
Key risk areas assessed included:

f Physical climate risks affecting e-bus
depots and charging infrastructure

f Electric vehicle (EV) battery-related
fire risks

f Road safety considerations

f Gender-Based Violence and Harassment
(GBVH) risks in public transport settings

f Supply chain risks associated with EV
components and infrastructure

f Water usage risks, particularly in regions
classified as high water stress zones
A key focus was evaluating Emergency
Preparedness and Response Plans (EPRP)
for on-road operations to ensure passenger
safety and operational resilience. This
approach reflects the Bank s commitment
to embedding strong E&S safeguards and
ensuring its clean mobility investments are
both sustainable and responsible.
Enhanced due diligence
in renewable energy
lending
Axis Bank has supported large-scale renewable
energy projects as part of its low-carbon
transition. Despite the sector s White
Category classification and limited regulatory
requirements, the Bank undertook rigorous due
diligence to identify and mitigate risks across the
project lifecycle.
Key assessment areas included the following:

f Labour and working condition risks within the
supply chain

f Biodiversity risks arising from project and
surrounding activities

f Land acquisition and involuntary resettlement
risks, including stakeholder grievances

f Community and stakeholder grievance
management mechanisms

f Potential microclimatic impacts such as the
albedo effect (changes in surface reflectivity)
The Bank conducted biodiversity assessments
using IBAT aligned with IUCN standards,
alongside detailed avian studies to assess species,
habitats, and environmental impacts. This
reflects Axis Bank s commitment to exceeding
regulatory norms and ensuring renewable
investments are environmentally responsible and
socially sustainable.

F. O R T H E E N V I R O N M E N T S 6

Scaling the green wholesale portfolio
We continue to strengthen our Wholesale
Banking portfolio by expanding lending
to sustainable sectors such as renewable
energy generation, urban mass transport,
electric mobility and green infrastructure.
Through this approach, the Bank aims to
support the development of low-carbon
and future-ready infrastructure while
aligning its financing strategy with broader
sustainability objectives.
Decarbonising our lending portfolio
In line with its internal targets, Axis Bank is progressively
moderating its exposure to carbon-intensive sectors such
as thermal power and coal within its Wholesale Banking
portfolio, thus actively aligning with India s Net Zero
aspirations. While thermal power is expected to continue
to play a role in supporting the country s economic
growth over the medium term, the Bank recognises that
India s long-term transition to a low-carbon economy
will be shaped by accelerated investments in renewable
energy, green hydrogen, carbon markets and clean
technology innovation.
In this context, Axis Bank remains committed to aligning
its financing approach with the evolving energy transition
landscape. The Bank also acknowledges that this
transition must be inclusive and equitable, ensuring that
vulnerable communities and sections of society are not
disproportionately impacted in the process.
1.4%
Percentage of exposure to thermal power generation
and coal mining, logistics and trading in the Bank s
total exposure (as of March 31, 2026)
Impetus to Retail EV financing
In line with its ESG commitments, the Bank has
established strategic partnerships with Original Equipment
Manufacturers (OEMs) to support the steady growth
of Electric Vehicle (EV) adoption. Reflecting its strong
ambition in this area, the Bank revised its EV lending
targets upward during the year.
The target share of EVs in the two-wheeler loan portfolio
has been increased from 6% to 8%, while the target share
in the four-wheeler passenger vehicle loan portfolio has
been raised from 4% to 7%, both by 2027 (measured as
the percentage share of EVs in rupee terms). This upward
revision underscores the Bank s commitment to enabling
cleaner mobility solutions and supporting India s transition
towards a lower-carbon transportation ecosystem.
22,400
EV loans offered in fiscal 2026
The Bank promotes EV adoption through a combination
of customer-focused offerings and strategic partnerships
with OEMs. A market-leading interest rate discount
of up to 0.5% for retail EV customers, coupled with
special arrangements with select OEMs and higher
payout structures, has helped strengthen the Bank s EV
financing proposition.
During the year, the Bank further deepened its engagement
with EV ecosystem participants, including dealers,
manufacturers and other stakeholders in the commercial
EV segment. These partnerships have enabled the Bank
to expand its reach and remain responsive to sectoral
developments such as advancements in battery technology
and changes in subsidy support.
Reflecting its continued confidence in the sector, the Bank
has also communicated to the ESG Committee its intention
to set fresh targets for EV financing across two-wheeler
and four-wheeler loan portfolios.
