4.
Provide the executive summary along with web-link(s) of Impact Assessment of CSR Projects carried out in
pursuance of sub-rule (3) of Rule 8, if applicable:

An impact assessment was conducted through a third-party agency on our eligible projects under Financial Literacy &
Financial Inclusion, Humanitarian & Relief, Healthcare & Nutrition, Environmental Sustainability and Education themes,
respectively, in accordance with sub-rule (3)(a) of Rule 8 of the Companies (Corporate Social Responsibility Policy)
Rules, 2014. A summary of these assessments is provided below, and the complete assessment reports are available on
the Bank s website at https://www.axis.bank.in/CSR.

Impact Assessment Project 1

Building Flood Resilient Community Program, Majuli, Assam | FY 2022-24

Axis Bank, in partnership with Ayang Trust, implemented a flood resilience and disaster risk reduction program
in Majuli Island, Assam to strengthen community preparedness and reduce flood vulnerabilities. The initiative
improved access to safe drinking water, sanitation, mobility, and emergency shelter while building local response
capacity. Key interventions included 20 raised handpumps, 10 community rescue shelters, 14 flood boats,
1,441 household water filters, and formation of 10 DRR committees, supported by a CSR outlay of `2.17 crores.

Board s Report
An OECD-DAC-aligned impact assessment using mixed methods (household surveys, interviews, focused group
discussion ( FGDs ), and observations) covered 101 stakeholders to evaluate relevance, effectiveness, sustainability,
and improvements in safety and preparedness.

Key Findings

1.
Safe Drinking Water Access: 80% of households reported daily access to safe drinking water during floods;
100% households received water filters.

2.
Flood Shelter Utilization: 83% respondents used community rescue shelters for safe shelter and sanitation.

3.
Evacuation Efficiency: 73% households reduced evacuation time to under 30 minutes due to accessible,
community-managed boats.

4.
Health Outcomes: Over 90% reported lower incidence of water-and flood-related diseases.

5.
Community Preparedness: 100% found DRR committee information timely and useful; 87% participated in
multiple mock drills.

6.
Migration Reduction: No households reported seasonal migration post intervention, indicating improved resilience.

Overall, the partnership has strengthened resilience in Majuli s high-risk riverine landscape by combining resilient
infrastructure with community-led systems. Families are safer, better connected, and more prepared during floods,
supported by improved WASH access and reduced health risks. Enhanced mobility and shelters enable quicker, safer
evacuation and recovery, while addressing gaps like shelter congestion can further improve emergency response. The
model shows strong potential for replication in similar flood-prone regions.

Impact Assessment Project 2

Supporting Pediatric Cardiac Surgeries Program | FY 2023-24

Axis Bank, in partnership with the Sri Sathya Sai Health & Education Trust, supported free pediatric cardiac surgeries
for children with congenital heart disease across Chhattisgarh, Haryana, and Maharashtra, ensuring end-to-end care
for economically vulnerable families. Between January and March 2024, 175 children were treated under the initiative,
supported by a CSR outlay of ₹2.62 crores. An OECD-DAC-aligned impact assessment using mixed methods (desk
reviews, 94 caregiver interviews, medical consultations, and case studies) evaluated relevance, effectiveness, efficiency,
impact, and sustainability, along with improvements in child health and family well-being.

Key Findings

1.
Clinical Success: 97% surgical success rate with 94% reporting no post-surgical complications.

2.
Child Health Improvement: 90% of caregivers reported significant improvement in children s health indicators such
as energy levels and physical activity.

3.
Education Outcomes: 90% school-age children who missed school days due to health-related challenges resumed
regular school.

4.
Financial Protection: 97% of families reported reduced medical expenses; 43% incurred zero post-surgery costs.

5.
Caregiver Livelihoods: 78% of caregivers reported a sharp reduction in workdays lost post-intervention.

6.
Equity & Reach: Majority of beneficiary families earned below `30,000 per month, indicating strong outreach to
vulnerable households.

Overall, the partnership demonstrates a high impact, equitable healthcare model addressing critical gaps in pediatric
cardiac care. IT has enabled rapid recovery, improved quality of life, and protected families from catastrophic
health expenditure. Children are able to resume normal routines quickly, reflecting strong program relevance and
impact. With robust clinical capacity and follow-up systems, the model shows strong scalability potential across
under-served regions.

S. TAT U T O R Y R E P O R T S
Integrated Annual Report 2025-26

Impact Assessment Project 3

Museum of Solutions Program, Mumbai | FY 2021-24

Axis Bank, in partnership with the JSW Foundation, supported the development of the Museum of Solutions
(MuSo)-India s first experiential children s museum promoting STEAM learning, sustainability, and alignment with
the UN SDGs through inclusive, child-centric design. The project enhanced learning through interactive exhibits and
hands-on activities, including for children with disabilities, supported by a ₹5 crores CSR grant for LEED-certified
infrastructure. An OECD-DAC-aligned impact assessment using mixed methods (desk reviews, field visits, surveys,
FGDs, and interviews) engaged over 150 stakeholders to evaluate relevance, effectiveness, and sustainability.

Key Findings

1.
Reach and Inclusion: Since inception, the museum recorded over 1.8 lakhs visitors, including more than
60,000 children and approximately 1,000 children with disabilities.

2.
Learning Engagement: 88% of children found exhibits very interesting, while 56% fully engaged in hands-on
learning activities.

3.
Learning Retention: 90% of children reported confidence in applying museum learnings in daily life and
classroom environments.

4.
User Satisfaction: 77% of children expressed very high satisfaction with the overall museum experience.

5.
Comparative Advantage: Among children who had visited other museums, 80% rated MuSo better in terms of
interactivity and quality of facilities.

6.
Sustainability and Outreach: 86% of children indicated willingness to recommend the museum to peers and family,
supporting sustained footfall and outreach.

Overall, the partnership with the JSW Foundation has enabled MuSo to deliver transformative, hands-on learning
that builds 21 century skills and complements formal curricula in an engaging, experiential environment. Its inclusive
design expands access for under-resourced schools and children with disabilities, ensuring equitable exposure to
STEAM and sustainability concepts. The museum s interactive approach allows children to learn sustainability by doing,
fostering deeper understanding and behavioural shifts. Introducing structured post-visit learning materials can further
strengthen knowledge retention and classroom integration. The model has strong potential to enhance and scale urban
learning ecosystems across India.

Impact Assessment Project 4

Axis Bank Shiksha Se Samriddhi Program | FY 2023-24

Axis Bank, in partnership with Microsave Consulting India, implemented Shiksha Se Samriddhi, a financial literacy
program in Muzaffarpur (Bihar) and Jeypore (Odisha) to build financial knowledge, confidence, and positive financial
behaviour through a blended classroom-plus-IVR model. The initiative reached 10,594 beneficiaries through
145 classroom sessions and 11 IVR modules, supported by flipbooks, flashcards, AV aids, and budgeting tools. Content
was persona-based and locally contextual, focusing on budgeting, savings, digital payments, and banking awareness.
With a CSR outlay of ₹2.10 crores, an OECD-DAC-aligned impact assessment using mixed methods (interviews,
FGDs, and KIIs) engaged 369 stakeholders to evaluate relevance, effectiveness, efficiency, impact, sustainability, and
behavioural change.

Key Findings

1.
Financial behaviour & budgeting: 79% participants now prepare household budgets (69% Bihar, 89% Odisha),
indicating improved financial planning.

2.
Digital payments adoption & household spillover: 77% encouraged at least one family member to adopt digital
payments; usage shifted from 100% cash to 69% cash with occasional digital use.

Board s Report

3.
IVR effectiveness & learning retention: 95% respondents reported IVR messages were clear and easy to
understand, validating the blended classroom-IVR delivery model.

4.
Financial awareness & confidence: 57% reported improved account awareness; 62% better understanding of
banking products; confidence gaps reduced (76% partially resolved, 24% fully resolved).

5.
Sustainability gap: Despite improved awareness, only 4% participants accessed government schemes or credit
linked to their bank accounts, highlighting the need for stronger scheme and product linkage support.

Overall, the partnership has driven measurable behaviour change in household financial management and digital usage,
especially among rural women. The blended model enhances retention, with IVR reinforcing learning beyond sessions.
Beneficiaries report greater confidence in engaging with formal banking, reducing reliance on informal channels. With
targeted support on UPI adoption, scheme access, and credit linkages, the program can deepen financial empowerment
and strengthen inclusion outcomes across low-income communities.

Impact Assessment Project 5

Solar Cold Storage System, Nagaland | FY 2022-24

Axis Bank, in partnership with the Investment & Development Authority of Nagaland ( IDAN ), supported
installation of solar-powered cold storage units across 16 remote, electricity-constrained locations in Nagaland
(December 2022-March 2024) to reduce post-harvest losses and improve market access for smallholder farmers. By
enabling storage of perishable produce, the project aimed to minimize spoilage, enhance price realization and stabilize
incomes, supported by a CSR outlay of ₹5.21 crores. An OECD-DAC-aligned impact assessment using mixed methods
(60 surveys and 10 interviews across four districts) evaluated relevance, effectiveness, efficiency, sustainability,
utilization and long-term viability.

Key Findings

1.
Post-harvest loss reduction: Earlier, 54.2% farmers reported losses of 26-50% and 6.3% above 50%; post
intervention, ~80% limited losses to 1-10%, with over 40% reducing losses below 5%.

2.
System reliability and performance: 91.7% of farmers reported no technical issues with the systems. Cooling
performance was rated good or excellent by more than 95% of users, reflecting strong operational reliability of the
solar-powered units.

3.
Storage capacity utilization: 81.3% of farmers never faced unavailability due to full capacity. Capacity constraints
were mainly observed at high-demand sites with smaller units (5 MT), particularly in Mokokchung.

4.
Income and market outcomes: Reduced spoilage and short-term storage (one-three days) enabled farmers to avoid
distress selling, improve market timing, and secure better price realization - leading to enhanced income stability.

5.
User satisfaction and sustainability: 86.7% of farmers indicated they would definitely recommend the facility,
showing high satisfaction, strong acceptance, and strong likelihood of sustained and repeated usage across seasons.

Overall, our partnership with IDAN has strengthened farm-level resilience by ensuring reliable cold-chain access
that reduces losses and improves incomes. The solar design delivers consistent performance in low-grid areas, while
community-managed operations foster ownership and effective utilization. With capacity expansion and hybrid backup
support, the model can deliver greater value and scale as a climate-resilient solution across similar geographies.

Impact Assessment Project 6

Axis Bank Financial Literacy for Youth (FLY) Program | FY 2022-24

Axis Bank, in partnership with the National Institute of Securities Markets ( NISM ), implemented the Financial Literacy
for Youth (FLY) Program to strengthen financial knowledge, responsible money management, digital finance awareness,
and career orientation among college students across India. Between February 2023 and March 2024, the program
reached over 37,000 students across 393 colleges in 17 states, covering budgeting, savings, investments, mutual funds,
digital payments, risk awareness, and careers in the securities market. The initiative was supported by a CSR outlay

S. TAT U T O R Y R E P O R T S
Integrated Annual Report 2025-26
of `2.46 crores. As required under CSR norms, an impact assessment using a mixed method approach aligned to the
OECD-DAC framework included 320 structured surveys and 10 in-depth interviews to assess relevance, effectiveness,
efficiency, impact, sustainability, and state-wise variations in outcomes.

Key Findings

1.
Financial knowledge and awareness: 69% of students correctly differentiated saving vs. investing, 74% identified
equity as a high-risk / high-return product, and 75% recognized SEBI as the market regulator, showing strong
conceptual gains.

2.
Behavioural change in money management: About 70% of students tracked expenses or budgeted, and
68% saved money in the last year, indicating early adoption of disciplined financial habits, especially in Haryana
and Uttar Pradesh.

3.
Digital finance usage and safety: Nearly 89% of students had opened a bank or payment account and 74% used
UPI or wallets regularly. Digital safety awareness was high, though 34% faced transaction issues, mainly in Bihar
and Madhya Pradesh.

4.
Career orientation and aspirations: Over 80% of students showed increased interest in finance-sector careers, and
72.5% were willing to pursue NISM certification, reflecting strong career motivation.

5.
Delivery quality and efficiency: Trainer quality and bilingual delivery were well appreciated, but short one-two
days sessions and large batch sizes in some colleges limited interaction and depth of learning.

6.
Sustainability and institutional acceptance: Students continued budgeting and saving after training, and colleges
were open to hosting future batches. However, low completion rates (27%) and delays in issuing certificates (~50%)
may affect long-term motivation.

Overall, the partnership with NISM has effectively equipped youth with practical money-management skills and strong
digital-safety awareness that they can apply immediately. Students demonstrated sharp increases in confidence and
curiosity toward finance-sector careers, reflecting meaningful aspiration shifts. Strengthening hands-on demonstrations
and adopting uncompressed training formats will enhance learning depth and retention. Streamlining the certification
process will reinforce credibility and improve employability signaling for participants. The program model is robust,
scalable, and well-suited for expansion across college campuses nationwide.

Impact Assessment Project 7

Miyawaki Tree Plantation Project, Navi Mumbai | FY 2021-24

Axis Bank, in partnership with Green Yatra Trust, implemented a Miyawaki urban afforestation initiative in Navi Mumbai
to restore degraded public green spaces and enhance biodiversity, climate resilience, and ecosystem services, aligned
with SDGs 11, 13, 15, and 17. Supported by a ₹1.5 crores CSR outlay, the project planted 25,000 saplings across
three municipal gardens using the high-density Miyawaki method to rapidly improve urban green cover. An impact
assessment, aligned with the IUCN NbS Global Standard and RBM framework, used mixed methods including field
surveys, biodiversity assessments, soil testing, and community interactions to evaluate ecological and social outcomes.

Key Findings

1.
Survival and establishment: Of the 25,000 saplings planted, approximately 19,450 survived, resulting in a weighted
survival rate of 77.8% after two years of maintenance, indicating strong establishment under urban conditions.

2.
Biodiversity enhancement: 56 species planted across four layers; four threatened species included; 30+ faunal
species recorded, showing early habitat recovery.

3.
Climate mitigation: Rapid biomass growth was observed within ~2.5 years, with canopy heights of 10-12 m
and average girths of ~30 cm. At mid-thinning stage ( 5 years), the combined carbon sequestration potential is
estimated at ~100,000 kg CO2 per year, demonstrating strong climate mitigation contribution.

4.
Ecosystem services: Miyawaki plots showed two-four times higher soil percolation rates and soil
temperatures 3-5 C lower than control areas, indicating improved groundwater recharge, urban cooling, and
microclimate regulation.

Board s Report

5.
Air-quality co-benefits: Analysis of nearby AQI data (2022-24) showed improvement in nine out of 12 months,
with more pronounced reductions during winter; while not solely attributable to the plantations, trends are
consistent with increased dense green cover.

6.
Economic efficiency: With a total CSR grant of `1.5 crores (including plantation, two years of maintenance
and monitoring), suggesting good value for money when assessed against ecological outcomes rather than
planting numbers alone.

7.
User access and governance: The plantations remain open, accessible public green spaces, implemented on
municipal land with formal approvals and handover to NMMC, supporting long-term stewardship and institutional
sustainability.

8.
Scalability and sustainability: Highly replicable Nature-based Solution aligned with urban climate and
biodiversity priorities.

Overall, the partnership has enabled rapid creation of dense, climate-resilient urban forests with strong ecological
outcomes. High survival, fast growth, and early biodiversity gains demonstrate effectiveness of the Miyawaki method.
The plantations are already delivering measurable ecosystem services - cooling, improved soil health, water percolation,
and air-quality benefits - enhancing urban livability. With robust governance and public accessibility, the model offers a
scalable, high impact solution for urban climate and biodiversity action.

Impact Assessment Project 8

Axis Sachetna (Financial & Health Literacy Program) | FY 2023-24

Axis Bank implemented short, local-language video modules on Financial and Health Literacy to improve women s
awareness on budgeting, savings, insurance, formal banking, and preventive health practices. The curriculum covered
budgeting, savings, DBT, RuPay, PMJDY, PMSBY, and PMJJBY, along with hygiene behaviours such as handwashing
and safe drinking water. Implemented by Quess Corp, the program reached 11.46 lakhs women across 20 states with
a CSR outlay of ₹25 crores. An OECD-DAC-aligned impact assessment using structured surveys (with proportionate
sampling and matched controls) evaluated knowledge recall, financial behaviours, insurance awareness, DBT usage,
and hygiene practices.

Key Findings

1.
High recall of financial messages: Over 88% of participants recalled core financial literacy messages, compared
to 15-16% among the control group. Recall of PMSBY insurance messaging was particularly high at 98.6%, versus
15.4% among control group.

2.
Improved household financial management: Nearly 89.7% of participants reported tracking household income
and expenditure, compared to 40.3% of control respondents. 64.8% began this practice after watching the video,
indicating strong attribution to the intervention. Similarly, 68.3% began estimating monthly savings post training,
while 62.7% of control respondents had no estimate of savings.

3.
Deeper engagement with formal banking: While savings account ownership was already high, participants
showed greater depth of engagement, with higher multi bank relationships (8.4% vs. 1.6%), significantly higher
awareness of RuPay debit card benefits (96% vs. 69.5%), and greater individual card ownership among women.

4.
Improved awareness and uptake of DBT: Only 3.7% of participants were unaware of DBT benefits, compared
to 28.1% of control group. 64.6% gained awareness after program exposure, and 86.2% reported receiving DBT
benefits, compared to 66.1% among control group, alongside far fewer don t know responses.

5.
Stronger insurance coverage: Nearly all participants (99%) recognised the importance of health and life insurance.
Comprehensive family health insurance coverage stood at 64.0% among participants, compared to 40.7% among
controls, driven largely by government schemes, particularly Ayushman Bharat. Life insurance coverage was also
higher (65.9% vs. 44.6%).

6.
Sustained health and hygiene outcomes: Recall of health and hygiene messaging was 92.5% among participants,
compared to 16.3% among control group. Over 90% agreed that the videos helped improve their lifestyle, with
strong reported adoption of preventive practices.

S. TAT U T O R Y R E P O R T S
Integrated Annual Report 2025-26

7.
Consistency across years: Findings reaffirm trends from earlier assessments, demonstrating sustained learning,
translation from awareness to action, and positive impacts on income stability, reduced health expenditure,
financial confidence, and community level spillovers.

Overall, the initiative has delivered strong improvements in women s financial behaviours, banking engagement, and
preventive health practices. High recall and clear behavioural shifts - well above control groups - demonstrate strong
attribution to the short video format. The program has strengthened financial confidence, improved DBT access, and
increased insurance uptake, contributing to greater household security. Health awareness has translated into sustained
behaviour change, making the model highly cost-efficient, scalable, and effective across geographies, with scope for
deeper impact through continued reinforcement and community dissemination.

Impact Assessment Project 9

Axis Bank Scholars Program (Ashoka University) | FY 2020-25

Axis Bank partnered with Ashoka University to implement the Ashoka Scholars Program, a ₹4.5 crores initiative
supporting access to high-quality higher education for 30 meritorious female undergraduate students across
14 states, with a focus on increasing women s participation in STEM and quantitative disciplines. Launched in 2020,
the program provides 50%-100% tuition support for students from economically constrained, often single-income
households, with selection based on rigorous needs assessment. An OECD-DAC-aligned impact assessment using
mixed methods (20 surveys and eight KIIs) evaluated relevance, effectiveness, equity, academic outcomes, and
socio-economic benefits. The report includes the latest cohort up to fiscal 2025.

The report is inclusive of the last cohort of students who progressed to the academic year of fiscal 2025.

Key Findings

1.
Critical Enabler of Access: The scholarship was essential for university entry, with most scholars confirming they
could not have attended Ashoka without financial aid. IT directly prevented educational drop-offs and ensured
deserving women entered a high quality academic environment.

2.
Significant Financial Stress Reduction: 56% reported moderate and 44% high reduction in financial burden;
families avoided loans and asset liquidation.

3.
Improved Academic Focus & Performance: Reduced financial pressure enabled stronger academic focus, and
research engagement; scholars reported clearer academic direction.

4.
Enhanced Professional Exposure & Opportunities: Students accessed high-value internships, research
assistantships, and industry projects that significantly strengthened employability. Such opportunities would not
have been accessible in most public institutions.

5.
Strong Psychosocial Transformation: Access to internships, research roles, and industry projects significantly
improved employability and made the students exponentially more confident.

6.
Promising Alumni Outcomes: Alumni outcomes show upward mobility - 64% employed, 36% pursuing higher
studies, with salary ranges between `7-22 lakhs per annum. These outcomes indicate strong long-term return on
investment and career readiness.

7.
Creation of Social Capital & Mentorship Networks: Students benefited from mentorship by faculty, peers, and
alumni, improving academic decision-making, career navigation, and long-term aspiration building. These networks
continue to support graduates beyond university.

8.
Identified Barriers in Application & Coverage: Short documentation windows (five-seven days), intrusive financial
disclosures, and technical access challenges posed entry barriers for disadvantaged applicants. Partial-aid scholars
(50-65% waiver) continued to face residual financial stress.

Overall, the partnership has enabled high-potential women to access world-class education otherwise unattainable,
significantly reducing financial stress and improving academic and career outcomes. Strong psychosocial gains and
mentorship networks support long-term mobility and impact. With refinements in application processes and increased
support for partial-aid students, the model demonstrates strong potential for scalable gender-equity outcomes.

Board s Report

Impact Assessment Project 10

Axis Bank Dementia Day Care Centre Program | FY 2022-24

Axis Bank, in partnership with Dignity Foundation, supports Dementia Day Care Centres ( DDCs ) in Pune and Delhi
NCR with a ₹1.09 crores outlay, providing structured, multidisciplinary care for elderly individuals with dementia while
offering respite to caregivers. Services include physiotherapy, occupational therapy, cognitive stimulation, recreational
activities, nutritious meals, and assisted transport. An OECD-DAC-aligned impact assessment using surveys, interviews,
and field evaluations examined relevance, effectiveness, efficiency, impact, and sustainability.

Key Findings

1.
Significant Reduction in Caregiver Burden: Caregivers gained approximately seven hours of daily respite
( 50 hours / week) as patients spent six-seven hours at the centre with safe pickup / drop services. This substantially
reduced continuous care giving pressure for nuclear families.

2.
High Satisfaction with Quality of Care: 100% of caregivers reported very high satisfaction with therapeutic care,
staff behaviour, hygiene, and overall environment. They especially appreciated the caregivers patience, empathy,
and respectful handling of dementia patients.

3.
Positive Improvements in Patient Health & Functionality: 67% of caregivers reported improvements in patients
mood, independence, treatment adherence, and physical activity. Patients were described as calmer, more
energetic, and more engaged in structured routines.

4.
Enhanced Emotional & Social Well-being: Patients showed improved emotional stability and reduced
irritability, supported by engaging activities such as brain games, music therapy, art and craft, group games, and
physiotherapy - strengthening cognitive and social stimulation.

5.
High Convenience & Accessibility for Families: Caregivers found the centre s location highly convenient, and the
pickup / drop service was seen as a major enabler, particularly for working caregivers balancing employment and
care giving responsibilities.

6.
Strong Therapeutic Value from Multidisciplinary Care: The structured combination of physiotherapy,
occupational therapy, cognitive stimulation, yoga / meditation, and social interaction aligned with dementia-care
best practices and the 4Rs - Reassure, Redirect, Respect, Routine.

7.
No Financial Stress Despite Program Fees: Although families spent `17,500 per month, all caregivers reported
that the program did not create financial strain. Many expressed willingness to pay more for expanded services,
including weekend shifts.

8.
Major Gaps in Communication & Documentation: Despite high satisfaction with care, 100% of caregivers were
dissatisfied with weak communication systems, irregular updates, inconsistent progress reports, and inadequate
documentation - identified as the program s biggest operational weakness.

Overall, the partnership with Dignity Foundation has established a high-impact dementia care model that improves
patient well-being while significantly reducing caregiver burden. The multidisciplinary approach enhances functionality,
mood, and social engagement, while reliable respite, safe transport, and supportive care enable families to balance
responsibilities. High caregiver satisfaction reflects strong delivery, though improving communication and reporting
systems can further strengthen effectiveness and trust.

Board s Report
Annexure 5
CONSERVATION OF ENERGY, TECHNOLOGY ABSORPTION, FOREIGN EXCHANGE EARNINGS AND OUTGO
[Pursuant to Section 134(3)(m) of the Companies Act, 2013 and Rule 8(3) of the Companies (Accounts) Rules, 2014]
Conservation of Energy
The steps taken by the Bank or impact on conservation of energy:
Availability of Electrical vehicle charging facility at large buildings i.e., Axis House, Mumbai, Andheri MIDC &
Axis House, Noida.
Lithium based battery backup is provided for all new UPS installations and existing replacement.
Inverter based air conditioning machines with green refrigerant gas (R32 / R410A) is provided for all new installations
and existing replacements.
Replacement of conventional lights with LED lights is provided in all branches / offices.
Centralized energy management system ( CEMS ) is operational in 600 branches to monitor and control
energy consumption.
Daily re-cycling capacity of 110 kiloliters ( KL ) of water through sewage treatment plant available at Axis House,
Mumbai.
Availability of annual rainwater harvesting capacity of approximately 2000 KL at Axis House, Mumbai.
Savings of water consumption by use of sensors / bio-blocks in urinals at selected large offices.
The steps taken by the Bank for utilising alternate sources of energy:
Solar power purchased under power purchase agreement ( PPA ) model for the Bank s business continuity centre
(data centre), Bengaluru.
Solar on ground installations aggregating 2MW.
Three large offices in Mumbai i.e., Axis House Mumbai, MIDC Andheri and the Ruby premises are operational on
100% renewal green energy.
Capital Investments on energy conservation equipment:
Replacement of conventional lights with LED lights at existing set up and new branches estimated at approximately
`3.55 crores per annum.
Yearly cost of approximately `3.22 crores towards centralised energy management services project.
Technology Absorption
1.
Efforts made towards Technology Absorption and benefits derived therefrom:

The Bank continues to advance technology led business initiatives to simplify and enhance customer banking
experience, supported by sustained innovation and adoption efforts during fiscal 2026.

Artificial Intelligence (AI): The Bank continues to strengthen its AI journey by advancing digital transformation
and innovation by enhancing customer service and technology delivery through AI enabled service capabilities,
integrated voice streaming, and strengthened compliance controls. The Bank is improving software delivery
efficiency through AI assisted development, enabling faster release cycles, higher code throughput, earlier risk
detection, and automated remediation. Through AI, the Bank intends to drive operational efficiency and revenue
growth by improving employee productivity; enable real time risk and compliance monitoring, accelerate business
responses, and empower instant, self service insights and query resolution. Furthermore, the Bank has integrated
Generative AI into its operations through the implementation of Axis Deep Intelligence (ADI), a Gen AI based
internal chatbot. Deployed at enterprise scale, ADI supports over 84,000 employees and resolves more than 81%
of employee queries at the branch level. Adoption has been strong, with an average of 33,000 monthly unique
users and nearly 32 lakhs queries processed.

S. TAT U T O R Y R E P O R T S
Integrated Annual Report 2025-26

OPEN Mobile Banking: The Bank continues to strengthen its Mobile Banking platform, which now provides
over 250 features. The platform has received strong customer endorsement, reflected in ratings of 4.8 on the iOS
App Store based on over 0.36 million reviews and 4.7 on the Google Play Store from more than 3.4 million reviews.
Ongoing enhancement of channel capabilities has driven a higher proportion of service requests through digital
channels, resulting in monthly active users exceeding 15 million. The Mobile Banking application has surpassed
35 million registered users. The Bank remains focused on sustaining its leadership position and maintaining higher
app store ratings through FY27.

Digital Payments: The Bank continued to deliver robust performance within the UPI ecosystem, supported by
a Virtual Payment Address (VPA) base of 2,448 million and the ability to process approximately 15.5 million
merchant transactions per day on the UPI platform. The Bank has retained the leadership position in the UPI
Payer Payment Service Provider (PSP) segment, with an estimated market share of 39%. The Bank also continues
to maintain lowest UPI technical decline rates among peer institutions, highlighting the strength, stability, and
reliability of its UPI infrastructure. Furthermore, the Bank has emerged as a leader in Central Bank for Digital
Currency ( CBDC ) with over 1 million registered customers and over 10 million transactions, and the CBDC
adoption has grown annually by ~50%. The WhatsApp banking platform now serves more than 30 million users,
executing 100+ campaigns and over 40 million communications. Additionally, the Bank strengthened its wholesale
and corporate banking digital capabilities through successful migration to a comprehensive Investment Banking
technology suite, launched key platforms and rolled out of Neo for Markets on mobile, and rationalisation of
channels within Neo for Corporates.

Lending Transformation: Adoption reached 94-100% in Bharat Banking products and 84% by volume in
Small Business Banking ( SBB ) Working Capital, supporting diversified business coverage across new, used,
salaried, and hybrid customer segments, with market share in Tractor & Farming Equipment increasing by
approximately 75% Y-o-Y.

Enterprise Core: The Bank continued to strengthen Finacle as the enterprise core, enabling scalable growth,
and higher levels of straight through processing across retail and corporate businesses. Platform modernization
and tighter digital integrations improved operational resilience, cost efficiency, and consistency of customer
experience across channels. The Bank achieved large scale branch transformation with Finacle branch upgrade
across 5,983 branches and 174 back offices migrated to the upgraded version, supporting asset and liability
services on the modernized platform.

The Bank expanded digital lending across Bharat Banking, SBB Working Capital, and Retail Home Loans,
achieving strong adoption across products. This enabled diversified coverage across new, used, salaried, and
hybrid customer segments, while strengthening market position in Tractor and Farming Equipment.

The Bank s upgraded Murex platform strengthened the resilience and scalability of treasury and markets
operations, aligned to the Platform and Resiliency strategy. Enhanced platform stability, controls, and integration
readiness enabled uninterrupted performance across market cycles.

Integration: The Bank has strengthened its API security framework through the implementation of
Aadhaar seeding controls, Enterprise Service Bus (ESB) level encryption, and Aadhaar Enabled Payment
System (AePS) compliance, supported by enhanced monitoring and integration of the API inventory with
change management processes. API adoption continues to scale, with approximately a 9% month on month
increase in request volumes, supported by over 951 partner APIs, more than 970 partner onboardings, and over
2,874 internal APIs. In parallel, the developer portal has expanded its reach, offering 650+ APIs to a growing
community of over 6,770 developers.

Enterprise Architecture and Cloud: The Bank strengthened its digital core by managing over 17 AI applications and
platforms to support key initiatives such as the Lending Transformation through co-solutioning microservices. The
CI / CD pipeline scaled to over 150 applications, while cloud DevOps achieved 99.99% availability, ensuring
compliance with the RBI and SEBI SBOM requirements. The Bank enabled AI supported software development
lifecycle (AI-SDLC) for over 35 applications. Furthermore, the Bank delivered outcomes by establishing an
enterprise grade open source cloud data Lakehouse, alongside automated vulnerability management and chaos
engineering to strengthen security and resiliency.

Board s Report

Infrastructure Resiliency: The Bank operates a centralized IT infrastructure across two primary data centers
located in different seismic zones in Mumbai and Bengaluru, along with a near disaster recovery (DR) data
center in Bengaluru. The data center architecture has been modernized and is now built for scale, with enhanced
capabilities, modular expansion provisions, and additional capacity and space to support future business growth
and technology adoption. These data centers are interconnected through wide area network connectivity and
multi protocol label switching (MPLS) to support application delivery across domestic and international branches.
The facilities adhere to industry benchmarking standards and are designed to maintain high availability. The
Bank s cluster based DR drills further ensure data privacy and security through encrypted and access controlled
failover mechanisms, DPDP compliant data handling practices, continuous threat monitoring, and preservation
of customer controlled security configurations.

Cyber Security: The Bank continues to uphold the highest standards of data security and remains focused on
strengthening its cyber security capabilities through ongoing investments. A comprehensive and holistic cyber
security program is in place, supported by a robust Cyber Security Policy and supporting standards aligned with
best practices and applicable regulatory requirements. The Bank s cyber security governance and operating
framework is based on the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) and ISO 27001 standards and is
structured around the core pillars of Govern, Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, and Recover.

The Bank maintains compliance with global information security and data protection standards, including
ISO 27001:2022 for Information Security Management Systems, ISO 27017:2015 for Cloud Security,
ISO 27034:2021 for Software Development and Application Security (Letter of Recognition), and Payment Card
Industry Data Security Standard (PCI DSS) version 4.0.1. The Bank is also certified under ISO 27018:2025, which
governs the protection of Personally Identifiable Information (PII) in cloud environments.

During the year, the Bank additionally achieved ISO / IEC 42001:2023 certification, the international standard
for Artificial Intelligence Management Systems (AIMS), reinforcing its commitment to responsible, secure, and
well governed adoption of AI technologies. This comprehensive certification framework underscores the strength
of the Bank s cyber security posture and the robustness of its security and AI governance controls across its IT
and application landscape. Axis Bank is the first bank globally to be certified under the ISO / IEC 42001 Artificial
Intelligence Management System standard.

The Bank operates 24x7 Security Operations Centre (SOC) and Cyber Security Operations Centre to ensure
continuous monitoring and response. All digital products of the Bank are actively monitored across the dark
net and deep web to detect potential threats and data exposure risks. The Bank consistently maintains a strong
BitSight security rating of 810 out of 900 (with 820 being the maximum achievable score), placing IT ahead of
Banking and Financial Services institutions tracked by BitSight and reflecting a well controlled and resilient
external security posture.

Cyber security control deployed by the Bank have resulted in: (a) Improved reliability and security of digital products
through enhanced controls and continuous monitoring, (b) Reduction in potential operational losses by preventing
cyber security incidents, minimizing recovery costs, and improving response efficiency, (c) Strengthened secure
product development practices aligned to global frameworks (NIST, ISO standards), ensuring security-by-design,
and, (d) Enhanced protection of customer data and trust through adherence to recognized security and data
privacy standards.

Management s Discussion and Analysis
MACRO-ECONOMIC ENVIRONMENT
The domestic economy was primed to enjoy a few more years of above-trend growth, given significant slack below the pre-
2020 growth trajectory, especially with a significantly slower pace of fiscal consolidation, easy monetary conditions, and
regulatory easing with a focus on ease of doing business. However, the West Asia conflict has thrown up a multi-dimensional
shock: commodity prices and availability. Uncertainty around business conditions, and higher global costs of capital are also
likely to be seen, driving greater caution and a holding back of consumption as well as investment activity. Nor is the end of
the conflict likely to fully reverse these trends, with developments in geopolitics suggestive of further conflicts where major
powers take sides down the road. All-in, these developments lead to reduced savings, higher costs of goods and services,
and higher costs of capital.
India Economy
The global landscape is likely to hit both long-term and short-term growth domestically. Short-term, tighter inputs and greater
uncertainty will stall otherwise strong recovery trends, while a less value chain friendly global environment with higher costs
of capital will likely hold back longer term trends. We already see continued financial outflows with high domestic savings
flowing out, in response to falling global savings and higher interest rates-these are likely a natural driver of raising domestic
rates to converge with global real levels, drive higher inflation, and lower long-term growth.
As against this, nimble policymaking and the use of countercyclical buffers can support both short and long-term growth. The
budget s focus on electronics and technology, accessibility to resource-rich areas, focus on biopharma and services exports
where tariff focus has been light, increased defence capex, and a focus on urban infra are all part of this mix, adding to the
existing focus on macro and micro-infrastructure. Additionally, India has also rapidly moved to close bilateral trade agreements
with complementarity as the focus - resulting in over 70% of exports now covered by deals. While the global environment will
likely remain inherently unpredictable, these steps will help build the foundation for stabler, well-positioned growth.
Banking system trends and linkages
Positive trends also reflect on the banking sector in general, and well-managed banks in particular: Stronger and higher quality
growth along with policy predictability have helped credit growth pick up over an increasingly diversified base - with MSMEs,
services, NBFCs and retail. Additionally, this growth has been towards higher quality assets, with innovations in appraisal
also better able to identify new avenues adding onto positive quality migration. Trends have resulted in continued declines in
industry credit costs and a high level of capital adequacy. Evidence from past input price shocks suggests a pickup in working-
capital linked credit in the medium term - though higher effective lending costs might hold up progress here.
Fiscal 2027 will also likely see good banking growth. Already, demand for debt capital has been strong, and tends to grow
further with higher input costs, as well as government measures to support credit quality through guarantee schemes. This is
added to by higher nominal growth, tracking past trends during periods of high inflation. Buffers in terms of capital are for now
adequate, but the time to look to rebuild these might come in at some point as conditions move away from the existent.
Medium-term growth
We have been championing the above trend growth seen with low inflation and imbalances indicative of domestic slack, with
GDP around 10% below pre-pandemic trends. However, this slack might be drained by the defacto financial tightening driven
by global developments, while higher prices and a weaker REER also imports inflation. To an extent, fiscal policy around
investments and productivity could raise long-term potential growth, while revenue spending in the form of subsidies and
other support measures can support the near term.
With these trends, we continue to see longer term growth of around 7%, but running hot above this now seems more challenging.
Management s discussion and analysis

S. TAT U T O R Y R E P O R T S
Integrated Annual Report 2025-26
Structured forward-looking drivers
We are looking at a less open world, with past periods of geopolitical competition seeing high commodity prices, high fiscal
spending, financial suppression, segmentation of value chains and financial flow channels, and lower productivity growth
especially for countries that stake out geopolitical neutrality. These trends impose less supportive global conditions and can
spur domestic measures to help mitigate conditions. However, absent these, growth domestically might slow notably with
reversal of some capital account measures and an increase in the government share of growth.

Management s Discussion and Analysis
NII constituted 68% of the operating revenue and increased by 3% from `54,348 crores in fiscal 2025 to `56,048 crores in
fiscal 2026. Yield on assets decreased by 61 bps primarily due to repricing of floating rate loans, linked to external benchmarks
rates such as repo as notified by RBI. Repo rate has decreased from 6.25% to 5.25%. Cost of funds also decreased by 30 bps. As
a result, the NIM decreased by 29 bps Y-o-Y to 3.69% in fiscal 2026.
During this period, the yield on interest earning assets decreased from 8.99% in fiscal 2025 to 8.38% in fiscal 2026. The yield on
advances decreased by 76 bps from 9.86% in fiscal 2025 to 9.10% in fiscal 2026. The decrease in yield on advances is mainly due
to decrease in repo rate and higher interest reversals due to higher slippages in fiscal 2026 as compared to fiscal 2025. ~73% of
the loans of the Bank are floating rate loans, linked to external/internal benchmark rates. The yield on investments decreased
by 19 bps during fiscal 2026.
Repricing of term and saving deposits during the course of fiscal 2026 has led to the decrease in cost of funds by 30 bps from
5.46% in fiscal 2025 to 5.16% in fiscal 2026. Cost of deposits decreased to 4.85% from 5.12% last fiscal mainly due to decrease
in funding cost of term deposits and savings deposits. Daily average CASA ratio as a proportion to deposits decreased by 146
bps in fiscal 2026 to 38% from 39% in fiscal 2025.
Performance of the Bank against the key drivers for the NIM improvement journey of the Bank in fiscal 2026 is as follows:

Improvement in Balance Sheet mix: loans and investments comprised 89% of total assets as at the end of fiscal 2026;

INR denominated loans comprised 96% of total advances at the end of fiscal 2026;

Retail and CBG advances comprised 67% of total advances as at 31 March 2026;

Balance outstanding in low-yielding priority sector shortfall deposits decreased by `5,761 crores Y-o-Y with priority
sector short fall deposits comprising 0.46% of total assets as at 31 March 2026 as compared to 0.90% at 31 March 2025;

Quality of liabilities at 31 March 2026 measured by outflow rate stood at 28.8%.
The Bank earned interest on income tax refund of `64 crores in fiscal 2026 as compared to `369 crores in fiscal 2025. The
receipt, amount and timing of such income depends on the nature and determinations by tax authorities and is hence neither
consistent nor predictable.

Retail Banking fees constituted 72% of the total fee income of the Bank in fiscal 2026 and grew strongly at 8% on a Y-o-Y basis.
Fees from retail cards grew 6% Y-o-Y in fiscal 2026 while retail non-card fees grew by 8%.
Fee income derived from the Wholesale Banking segment has increased Y-o-Y at 10% of the Bank s total fee income. Within
Wholesale Banking, granular transaction banking fees grew 10% Y-o-Y. Fee income from the Bank s CBG (Commercial Banking
Group) that lends to small and medium enterprises accounted for 3% of the Bank s total fee income for fiscal 2026 and fiscal
2025 and grew strongly at 17% Y-o-Y.
During fiscal 2026, trading profits without considering impact of mark-to-market gain/(loss) on revaluation of investments
increased by 66% to `2,250 crores from `1,359 crores last year mainly on account of higher profits on the SLR portfolio and
foreign exchange gain. This is after netting off the MTM losses on forex and derivative contracts in fiscal 2026 due to impact of
tightening of foreign exchange rules regarding net open position, non-deliverable forwards etc. by RBI.
The Bank recognised mark-to-market loss on revaluation of investments of `875 crores in fiscal 2026 as compared to a mark-to-
market gain on revaluation of investments of `701 crores in fiscal 2025, due to headwinds in markets at year end arising from
geopolitical conflicts.
The Bank s miscellaneous income in fiscal 2026 stood at `313 crores compared to `694 crores in fiscal 2025, comprising mainly
income from display of publicity material amounting to `185 crores as compared to `258 crores in fiscal 2025.
Operating revenue
The operating revenue of the Bank increased by 3% to `82,179 crores from `79,605 crores last year. The core income streams
(NII and fees) constituted 98% of the operating revenue, reflecting the stability of the Bank s earnings.

Management s Discussion and Analysis
On 21 November 2025, the Government of India consolidated 29 existing labour laws into a unified framework of four Labour
Codes (including the Code on Social Security, 2020), collectively referred to as the New Labour Codes . Since Q3 of fiscal
2021, based on a prudent internal policy, the Bank has been consistently provisioning for gratuity liability, in anticipation of the
implementation of the Code on Social Security, 2020. In Q3 of Fiscal 2026, the Bank performed a preliminary assessment of the
financial impact of the New Labour Codes based on the draft Central Rules and FAQs published by the Ministry of Labour and
Employment, in line with the guidance from the Institute of Chartered Accountants of India and charged to its Profit and Loss
Account an amount of `25.44 crores towards gratuity, primarily due to changes in the wage definition. The Bank will continue
to monitor the finalization of Central and State rules relating to the New Labour Codes and adjust its estimates and provisions
in subsequent reporting periods for gratuity and other aspects of the New Labour Codes, in accordance with applicable
accounting standards.
Other operating expenses increased by 7% from `25,307 crores in fiscal 2025 to `27,096 crores in fiscal 2026. The increase
is primarily due to statutory expenses attributable to PSLC, CSR and DICGC premium. There has also been an increase in
volume linked costs coming from rising business volumes and investments in technology to support future business growth.
Rate of increase in expenses has moderated in fiscal 2026 to 5% as compared to 6% in fiscal 2025.
The Operating Expenses to Assets ratio decreased by 18 bps to 2.28% for fiscal 2026 as compared to 2.46% last year.
Operating profit
During fiscal 2026, the operating profit of the Bank increased by 2% to `42,817 crores from `42,105 crores last year on account
of steady growth in operating revenues, partially offset by a moderated growth in operating expenses.

During fiscal 2026, provisions (other than provisions for tax) increased 71% Y-o-Y to `13,263 crores from `7,759 crores last
year. Key items of provisions are explained below -
Provisions for non-performing assets and Recoveries from written off accounts:
The gross slippages increased from `19,474 crores in fiscal 2025 to `24,577 crores in fiscal 2026. Prudent application of
technical parameters for recognizing slippages and consequent upgrades has led to the additions of `7,158 crores to gross
NPAs and `2,809 crores to net NPAs for the year ended 31 March 2026 ( Technical Impact ). Technical Impact is largely
restricted to cash credit and overdraft products and one-time settled accounts. The Bank provided `13,026 crores towards
non-performing assets compared to `11,356 crores last year.
The Bank s recoveries from written off accounts in fiscal 2026 were lower, amounting to `3,542 crores as against `3,809
crores in fiscal 2025.
Other provisions

Provisions for standard assets:

The Bank made a provision of `2,049 crores for standard assets including unhedged foreign currency exposure compared
to `11 crores last year.

S. TAT U T O R Y R E P O R T S
Integrated Annual Report 2025-26

During fiscal 2026, the Bank made a provision for standard assets of `657 crores as against a provision of `66 crores made
in fiscal 2025 on account of increase in advances.

During fiscal 2026, the Bank also made a provision of `161 crores in fiscal 2026 as compared to write-back of `55 crores
made in fiscal 2025 for unhedged foreign currency exposure.

During the year ended 31 March 2026, following an RBI advisory, post its FY25 annual inspection, the Bank in Q2FY26
made an additional one-time standard asset provision of `1,231 crores for declassified PSL loans. The customer terms
remain unchanged. This provision will be written back to the P&L upon all the declassified loans being made compliant,
recovered or closed in the normal course, not later than March 31, 2028. No divergence in asset quality or NPA
provisioning was identified in the said annual inspection.

During fiscal 2026, the Bank had a write-back in provision `18 crores in fiscal 2026 as compared to write-back of `38
crores in fiscal 2025 pursuant to implementation of resolution plan in certain accounts.

During fiscal 2026, there was a write-back of `195 crores in provision for loans subjected to COVID-19 and MSME
restructuring mainly on account of slippages and recoveries, as compared to a write back in provision of `143 crores
in fiscal 2025.
Provisions for other contingencies:

During fiscal 2026, the Bank took the initiative to further strengthen its balance sheet by voluntarily enhancing its prudent
provisioning framework for standard assets. In line with this framework, an additional one-time provision of `2,001 crores
was made during the quarter. This measure is entirely prudent and does not indicate any concerns regarding asset quality
or adverse credit developments in the Bank s loan or investment portfolio as of the reporting date.
As at the end of fiscal 2026, the cumulative non-NPA provisions held by the Bank amounted to `15,473 crores with a
standard asset coverage ratio (all non-NPA provisions / standard assets) of 1.26%.
Provision for tax for fiscal 2026 stood at `5,097 crores as compared to `7,973 crores for last year.
In the fiscal year 2023, the Bank acquired Citibank s India Consumer Business from Citibank N.A. (acting through its branch in
India) ( CBNA ) and the NBFC Consumer Business from Citicorp Finance (India) Limited ( CFIL ) collectively referred to as Citi
India Consumer Business on a going concern basis. In accordance with an independent valuer s report, intangibles (excluding
goodwill) amounting to `8,714.24 crores were recognised in the Bank s financial statements. Despite retaining access to and
business use of these assets, as a prudent measure aimed at protecting its capacity to pay dividends, the Bank opted to fully
amortise these intangibles through the Profit and Loss account in Fiscal 2023. Furthermore, the Bank elected not to create
any deferred tax asset in Fiscal 2023 on such intangibles, nor did the Bank consider the deductibility on the said intangibles
while providing for current tax in the books until the regular tax assessment for the said financial year was completed. During
the year ended 31 March 2026, following the conclusion of regular assessment proceedings by the income tax authorities, tax
depreciation on these intangibles was allowed. As a result, the tax expense for Fiscal 2026 is lower by `2,193.20 crores, which
includes the reversal of excess tax provisions made in prior years amounting to `1,129.80 crores, reduction of current year s tax
expense by `265.85 crores and recognition of a deferred tax asset of `797.55 crores.
Net profit
Net profit for fiscal 2026 decreased by 7% Y-o-Y to `24,457 crores as compared to the net profit of `26,373 crores last year.
Asset Quality Parameters
The Bank added `24,577 crores to Gross NPAs during fiscal 2026 with the ratio of Gross NPAs to gross customer assets
declining to 1.23% at the end of March 2026 from 1.28% as at the end of March 2025. The Bank added `14,009 crores to
Net NPAs after adjusting for recoveries and upgradations of `3,109 crores and `7,459 crores, respectively and the Bank s Net
NPA ratio (Net NPAs as percentage of net customer assets) increased marginally to 0.37% from 0.33%. Prudent application
of technical parameters for recognizing slippages and consequent upgrades has led to the additions of `7,158 crores to gross
NPAs and `2,809 crores to net NPAs. The Bank s provision coverage ratio excluding prudential write-offs during the fiscal
stood at 70%. The Bank s accumulated prudential write-off pool stood at `44,631 crores as at end of fiscal 2026.

of gross customer assets. There is no outstanding with respect to

Total assets increased by 17% to `18,86,850 crores as on 31 March 2026 from `1,609,930 crores as on 31 March 2025,
driven by 19% growth in advances and 12% growth in investments.
Advances
Total advances of the Bank as on 31 March 2026 increased by
19% to `12,33,570 crores from `10,40,811 crores as on 31
March 2025. Retail advances comprised 55% of total advances
and grew by 8% to `6,73,468 crores, corporate advances
comprised 33% of total advances and grew by 38% to `4,12,943
crores and SME advances constituted 12% of total advances and
grew by 24% to `1,47,159 crores.
Domestic advances of the Bank as on 31 March 2026 grew
by 18% to `11,88,744 crores from `1,011,101 crores as on
31 March 2025. Further, domestic corporate advances of the
Bank as on 31 March 2026 increased by 36% to `3,70,028 crores from `2,71,471 crores as on 31 March 2025.
The retail lending growth was led by Small Banking Business (SBB) and Bharat banking loans (rural lending). Home loans
remain the largest retail segment and accounted for 26% of retail loans, rural lending (Bharat Banking) 15%, loans against
property (LAP) 13%, personal loans (PL) and credit cards(CC)
were 19%, auto loans 9% and Small Business Banking (SBB)
at 12%, while non-schematic loans comprising loan against
deposits and other loans accounted for 6%.
Investments
The investment portfolio of the Bank grew by 12% to `4,45,033
crores. Investments in Government and other approved
securities, increased by 21% to `3,73,899 crores. Other
investments, including corporate debt securities, decreased by
19% to `71,134 crores. 81% of the government securities have
been classified in the HTM category, while 25% of the bonds and
debentures portfolio has been classified in the AFS category.
Other Assets
Other assets of the Bank as on 31 March 2026 increased to `96,795 crores from `66,953 crores as on 31 March 2025,
primarily on account of increase in Mark-to-Market (MTM) asset on forex and derivative contracts amounting to `48,477
crores as on 31 March 2026 as compared to `20,496 crores as on 31 March 2025 which was partially offset by a decrease in
Priority Sector Shortfall deposits from `14,450 crores as on 31 March 2025 to `8,690 crores on 31 March 2026.

Shareholder s funds
Shareholder s funds of the Bank increased from `1,78,617 crores as on 31 March 2025 to `2,04,194 crores as on 31 March
2026. This is mainly on account of profits earned during fiscal 2026.
Deposits
The total deposits of the Bank increased by 14% to `13,35,834
crores against `11,72,952 crores last year. Savings Bank
deposits reported a growth of 11% to `3,44,136 crores, while
Current Account deposits reported increase of 11% to `1,84,776
crores. As on 31 March 2026, low-cost CASA deposits stood
at `5,28,912 crores and constituted 40% of total deposits.
On a daily average basis, Savings Bank deposits, increased by
6% to `3,02,567 crores, while Current Account deposits grew
by 8% to `1,38,582 crores. The percentage share of CASA in
total deposits, on a daily average basis, was at 38% compared
to 39% last year.
Borrowings
The total borrowings of the Bank increased by 28% from `1,84,147 crores in fiscal 2025 to `2,35,271 crores in fiscal 2026.
The Bank has issued debt instrument amounting to `5,000 crores in the form of long-term bonds against Infrastructure and
affordable housing (Infrastructure bonds) in India.
Other Liabilities and provisions
Other liabilities of the Bank increased by 51% over the year to `1,10,209 crores as on 31 March 2026 from `73,106 crores
as on 31 March 2025, which is mainly on account of increase in Mark-to-Market (MTM) liability on forex and derivative
contracts to `45,827 crores as compared to `19,929 crores last year.

Capital Management
The Bank continues its endeavour for greater capital efficiency and shoring up its capital adequacy to enhance
shareholder value.
The Bank s overall capital adequacy ratio (CAR) under Basel III stood at 16.42% at the end of fiscal 2026, well above the
benchmark requirement of 11.50% stipulated by Reserve Bank of India (RBI). Of this, the Common Equity Tier I (CET I) CAR
was 14.38% (against minimum regulatory requirement of 8.00%) and Tier I CAR was 14.78% (against minimum regulatory
requirement of 9.50%). As on March 31, 2026, the Bank s Tier II CAR under Basel III stood at 1.64%.
The organic business of the Bank consumed 29 bps (net) of CET I in fiscal 2026.

continues to be strong and is sufficiently robust for IT to pursue growth opportunities with adequate liquidity buffers.

Management s Discussion and Analysis
BUSINESS OVERVIEW
In 2019, we launched our Growth Profitability and Sustainability strategy, also known as the House of GPS . Over the years,
as part of annual reviews of the House of GPS, we have added areas of distinctiveness, key themes for the Bank to focus on
and other changes to reflect the priorities of the Bank and accounting for the changing environment. The House of GPS, as IT
stands today, continues to reflect our aspirations and remain relevant. Our overall strategy, and specific business and function
strategies, are aligned with our core philosophy of GPS -

Growth: Accelerate deposits growth, focus on profitable advances, achievement of leadership positions across our focus
areas, drive One Axis-led growth across the Group and scale-up of subsidiaries and Axis Digital Bank

Profitability: Focus on profitability and NIM, growth in fee income, improve operating efficiency, optimise costs,
strengthen collections infrastructure and maintain control over credit cost

Sustainability: Strengthen governance across the Group with an integrated assurance approach to enhance risk
management, robust audit and compliance culture, retain high-quality talent, strengthen underwriting, technology and
analytical capabilities and a robust operations platform
As part of the GPS initiatives, under the One Axis vision, we are focused on creating a one-stop solution for banking needs by
integrating the strengths of the subsidiaries with the Bank.
Focus on granular, risk-adjusted, higher-yielding segments has enabled us to deliver on our ROE aspirations and control
credit costs. Our best-in-class mobile banking application, digital/analytical capabilities, and emerging technology, such as
generative AI, gives us the right to win in digital banking. The Bank focuses on reimagining end-to-end journeys, transforming
the core, and becoming a partner of choice for ecosystems. While the macro-economic environment continues to evolve
amidst geo-political uncertainties, India remains a bright economic spot, giving us an incredible opportunity to tap into the
growing capex / infrastructure/investment boom. We have a self-sustaining capital structure adequate to fund organic
growth. Our transformation projects have enabled us to reach closer to our GPS ambitions - Neo (offers end-to-end digital
journeys with DIY onboarding, for banking as well as beyond-banking needs of MSMEs), Siddhi (a super app that empowers
our colleagues to engage seamlessly with customers), granularisation and premiumisation of liability franchise, focus on cost
optimisation, drive collection transformation and set up next generation collection architecture for the Bank. We have also
started yielding visible results due to our investments in long-term distinctiveness drivers - Axis 2.0 (becoming India s
Best Tech Bank), Bharat Banking (tapping the high growth potential in rural and semi-urban markets), Sparsh (customer
obsession program to aid improvement in NPS rankings).
We remain committed to our GPS strategy of working towards Growth, Profitability and Sustainability over the medium-
term and aim to become a resilient, all-weather franchise.
Retail Banking
Over the past decade, the Bank has built a strong and scalable Retail Banking franchise that remains a core pillar of its growth
strategy. Anchored in a customer-first philosophy, differentiated product capabilities, and an extensive phygital distribution
network, the Retail franchise continues to address the evolving financial needs of a diverse customer base across life
stages and segments.
The Bank s Retail Banking business offers a comprehensive suite of products and services spanning deposits, payments, wealth
management, and lending, catering to retail customers, small businesses, non-resident Indians (NRIs), and retail institutions.
These offerings are supported by increasingly digital-first, secure, and intuitive platforms that enhance convenience,
accessibility, and customer engagement. During fiscal 2026, the Retail Banking segment contributed 55% to the Bank s
advances and 72% to the Bank s fee income.
On the liabilities side, the Bank provides a wide range of solutions, including savings and current accounts, term deposits
(fixed and recurring), and customised deposit products tailored to varying customer preferences and liquidity requirements.
Retail lending spans home loans, loans against property, automobile and two-wheeler loans, commercial vehicle loans,
personal loans, gold loans, education loans, credit cards, small business banking loans, and agriculture loans, among others
enabling the Bank to participate across a broad spectrum credit demand segments.

S. TAT U T O R Y R E P O R T S
Integrated Annual Report 2025-26
Beyond core banking products, the Retail Banking franchise offers an integrated ecosystem of services, including debit and
credit cards, forex cards, bill payments, and wealth management solutions. The Bank also distributes third-party financial
products such as mutual funds, life and non-life insurance, Government bonds, and other investment products, enabling
customers to meet their savings, investment, and protection needs through a single, trusted platform.
The Retail strategy continues to focus on deepening customer relationships by increasing wallet share, acquiring high-
quality new customers, and delivering a consistently superior customer experience. Central to this strategy is a lifecycle-
based engagement model, supported by advanced data analytics and technology capabilities that enable deeper customer
insights, targeted engagement, and timely, relevant product offerings. During fiscal 2026, the Bank accelerated its digitisation
agenda across sales, service, and branch operations, leveraging automation, analytics, and streamlined processes to enhance
operational efficiency and turnaround times. These initiatives are driving a more seamless, intelligent, and responsive banking
experience across channels.
With a strong liability franchise, a diversified product portfolio, and a robust digital and analytics backbone, the Bank believes
IT is well positioned to sustainably capture long-term growth opportunities in India s retail financial services landscape.
Retail Deposits
The Bank s deposit franchise continued to strengthen during fiscal 2026, supported by robust customer acquisition funnels,
an expanded distribution footprint, differentiated product propositions, and sustained momentum in the Salary and
Burgundy segments.
The Existing-to-Bank (ETB) franchise exhibited steady growth over the past year, with the ETB Savings Account (SA) book
increasing by 3.13% since March 2025, led by broad-based growth across segments. Ongoing process improvements and
digitisation initiatives enabled a reduction of over 50% in turnaround time for SA account opening, significantly enhancing
the customer onboarding experience. Further, the introduction of a personalised engagement calendar through Siddhi for
quarterly customer connects improved overall customer contactability to 80%. The Bank also delivered a 5% improvement in
servicing through Straight Through Processing (STP), driven by targeted product enhancements such as facial authentication.
The Bank continues to sharpen its strategic focus on deposit premiumisation, with an emphasis on increasing the share of
premium accounts in new acquisitions and deepening engagement with affluent and emerging affluent customer segments.
This calibrated approach involves strengthening premium product propositions while progressively moderating dependence
on lower-variant offerings in metro and urban markets.
This strategic repositioning reflects evolving market dynamics and underscores the Bank s objective of enhancing profitability,
strengthening brand differentiation, and improving the overall quality of the deposit franchise. The Bank remains focused
on expanding its premium product suite, leveraging technology-led capabilities, and delivering superior, seamless customer
experiences to support sustainable long-term growth.
In parallel, the Bank has undertaken targeted process
simplification initiatives aimed at eliminating friction points,
strengthening end-to-end digital journeys, and enhancing
service capabilities across channels, including phone banking.
These measures have contributed to improved customer
outcomes and a reduction in service-related complaints. As at
the end of fiscal 2026, the Bank maintained a stable CASA mix,
with CASA deposits constituting 40% of total deposits.
The Bank also continued to enhance its corporate salary and
worksite engagement model, reimagining worksites beyond the
traditional helpdesk approach. Strategic brand partnerships with
leading electric two-wheeler manufacturers, multinational electronics companies, and consumer durable players enabled
deeper employee engagement and strengthened presence across corporate ecosystems and induction programs.
The Bank remains committed to strengthening its fraud prevention framework and regulatory safeguards, supported
by continued investments in robust onboarding controls. Customer onboarding processes have been fortified through
